A full visit takes several hours and it worth to take a break at one of the Cafe to fully enjoy the experience.

The gift shop is a nice way to finish your visit and they have some lovely gifts that are very unique.

As Definitive an Art Museum There Is. It seems ludicrous to write a review about the Louvre, but I've done dumber things. Like writing a review about Nothing, Arizona, when there was literally nothing. The opposite is true here as the Louvre is everything an art museum should be. With over 35,000 pieces and over nine miles of exhibition space, it can be overwhelming, but some pre-planning helped make the most of the three-plus hours my husband and I spent here.

First things first, buy time-stamped tickets as soon as you know when you'll be in Paris. That's what we did, and it allowed us to go at the earliest time possible to avoid the worst of the crowds. Check to see if the Porte des Lions is open. Far less used than the glass pyramid, that's the side entrance closest to the Denon Wing where most of the famous works are. Currently Google Maps states it's temporarily closed, but it's worth checking.

Well before coming here, you should decide what the essential works are you want to see. Meandering the Louvre without a plan will tucker you out, and seating is at a premium. My husband and I wanted to see the acknowledged Top 10 works on a navigational path that allowed us time to browse with minimal backtracking. We started on Level 1 of the Denon Wing and sprinted to Room 711. That's where Leonardo da Vinci's "Mona Lisa" hangs.

It's small, under glass, and you can see it only from a restricted distance. But believe me, the crowd you see first thing in the morning reflects the best time to check this masterpiece off your list. Flip around and enjoy the museum's largest painting, "The Wedding Feast in Cana" by Veronese. Head to the back of the room and make a right to approach "The Winged Victory of Samothrace" in Room 703, a truly majestic sight.

This was where Audrey Hepburn floated down the steps in her red gown and gossamer shawl in "Funny Face". Walk beyond the mythic statue and make a right into the glorious Room 705 where you get up close to the resplendent French Crown Jewels. Walk back out and head to Room 702 where you see "The Coronation of Napoleon", a massive historical recreation by Jacques-Louis David. Continue to Room 700 and see two masterpieces.

The uplifting "Liberty Leading the People" by Eugene Delacroix and the darkly tragic "The Raft of the Medusa" by Theodore Gericault. Go downstairs to Room 403 on Level 0 still in the Denon Wing. That's where we saw the beautiful neoclassical sculpture, "Psyche Revived by Cupid's Kiss" by Antonio Canova. Walk over to Room 345 and join the admiring crowd ogling the Venus de Milo.

At this point, we allowed ourselves to roam among the Roman, Greek, and Egyptian antiquities. It's an expansive space and hard to maneuver if you're looking for pieces in particular. We found the Great Sphinx of Tanis in Room 338 of the Sully Wing and eventually the Code of Hammurabi from ancient Mesopotamia in Room 227. It's as mythic as the Rosetta Stone. We recommend strolling through the sculpture courtyard.

It was quite a collection of magnificent pieces in an atrium-like setting. We weren't aware there was an Angelina on the premises, and it wasn't the zoo the original one is off the Tuileries Gardens. We managed to enjoy their fabled hot chocolate and a light brunch without waiting for a table. We did all of that in a little over three hours and still felt there was so much more to discover and explore. Yes, the Louvre is the opposite of Nothing.

Title: An architectural and cultural jewel, dimmed by modern excesses. Visiting the Louvre is undoubtedly a profound and humbling experience. The grandeur of the château, with its centuries-old history, combined with the modernity of the iconic glass pyramid, creates a stunning architectural dialogue between past and present. The moment one steps into the Cour Napoléon, a sense of awe takes over — the sheer scale of the museum, the elegance of the façades, and the brilliance of the glass structure make one feel both inspired and infinitesimal. In terms of artistic content, the Louvre remains unrivaled. It is, without question, one of the greatest museums on the planet. From the enigmatic smile of the Mona Lisa to the majestic Winged Victory of Samothrace and the timeless beauty of the Venus de Milo, every corner of this institution breathes the essence of human creativity.

However, such magnificence deserves an infrastructure worthy of its stature — and here, unfortunately, the Louvre reveals some critical flaws.

Despite timed entries, the number of visitors admitted each day far exceeds the museum's comfortable capacity. The result is a constant crowd, making fluid circulation almost impossible in the most popular galleries. Visitors are often herded like cattle through narrow passages, with little opportunity to stop, reflect, and truly appreciate the artworks. The time slots allocated for visits are insufficient and lead to an overwhelming experience rather than an enriching one.

From an administrative standpoint, the management of security raises serious concerns. Recent incidents involving vandalism of priceless artworks for ideological reasons demonstrate a lack of both surveillance and trained personnel. The few security agents present seem more focused on maintaining order than preserving the sanctity of the exhibits. A cultural institution of this scale and renown should never allow such vulnerabilities to exist.

Commercially, the Louvre houses a variety of boutiques and cafés, which at first glance add to the visitor experience. However, upon closer inspection, it becomes clear that these are driven by a purely commercial logic. Souvenirs — such as keychains or simple postcards — are sold at exorbitant prices, often five or six euros for a modest trinket. This undermines the educational mission of the museum and suggests a troubling prioritization of profit over accessibility and cultural sharing.

In terms of transportation, the museum is excellently served by multiple options: buses, taxis, and metro lines. The underground Carrousel du Louvre entrance via the Palais Royal – Musée du Louvre station is a clever and pleasant solution for accessing the museum while avoiding the crowds above ground. Nonetheless, as with the museum itself, a greater degree of security and supervision within these transit areas would be welcome.

Lastly — and perhaps most disheartening — is the behaviour of certain tourists. The Louvre has, for many, become nothing more than a backdrop for social media content. The obsession with selfies and photography has eclipsed genuine engagement with art. It is increasingly rare to find visitors quietly contemplating a painting; instead, one is surrounded by a sea of arms holding smartphones, flashes going off, and people pushing forward not to admire, but to capture a fleeting image. Art, in such an environment, becomes secondary — a mere tool for online visibility.

🤩. Impressive experience! The Louvre Museum is truly unique, with countless works of art and amazing atmosphere. We already had our tickets and we waited at the lower entrance, in the queue from the beginning of the mall, but the process went very fast and we entered without delay.

Unmissable if you are visiting Paris. For history lovers, this museum is stuffed with incredible antiquities. I'd actually say it's better than the British Museum. We managed to see most of it over a four hour period. The layout can be confusing and the staff are unbelievably rude. I was really polite and got sneered at. But that aside, it's a joy to go from ancient Egypt to 18th century France and so on. Don't obsess with the Mona Lisa - there really is more to see than that. The queueing system actually worked.

From the pyramid to painting. You will get lost 100 times but it will be worth it! Too much to see for a single visit especially if you are not great walkers. Tragic people that you have to take selfies (a tide then) in front of the Mona Lisa

Unforgettable. It is one of the best travel experiences qie did. Indescribable!!! There's no way not to be delighted and take a bath of culture.

Lucky Louvre Visit. The Louvre was not on our bucket list to visit. Reading reviews and talking to others who had been left us with the impression there was no chance of viewing the artwork up close due to the massive crowding. Well, the art gods must have been smiling down on us. We had a full day tour to Versailles canceled by the operator, so we pivoted and took a chance on the Louvre. We basically walked right up, hardly no line, bought a 20 euro entry ticket and strolled in. Virtually no crowds. Able to view each masterpiece up close and personal including the world famous ladies, Mona and Venus. But the multitude of other ancient and intriguing pieces should not be overlooked. The sheer age and artistry of the pieces housed here are breathtaking. We feel incredibly lucky that on the day of our visit, the museum felt like it was ours alone.

Unforgettable tour. We had a day and a half to visit the museum. Very little time. You lose a good time in the queue (that we had already bought a few months before), and there inside, in addition to having hundreds of people, which makes it difficult to walk, even more with a child of 4 years together, there is a lot to see for those who like this type of tour. My wife and I carried the baby and circulated a lot. The Egyptian wing is fantastic (which I most wanted to see) and my wife to Monalisa (this with dozens of people around her). And circulating we end up arriving in various paintings, famous sculptures - Write sitting, Victory of Samotrácia, etc. It takes at least 2 days to see this museum.

Louvre Couture was spectacular. Fashion exhibit was simply stunning! The special Louvre Couture exhibit was phenomenal. We were there on the last planned day (it was later extended I understand) and I hope they bring something like this back. The museum is always worth a visit (make sure to book in advance and plan for perhaps two days. Use the Richelieu or Carousel entrances for smoother entry on your timed tickets. For the fashion exhibit, they had couture pieces integrated into the historic setting of the palace. the designers were inspired by the setting so it was an elegant match. Please take your time to make a lengthy visit to enjoy parts of the museum that are less crowded (Richelieu wing among them) and make your way to the old medieval foundation of the palace.

great !!!. The Louvre Museum in Paris is a museum of worldwide renown – and yet this place manages to impress not only by its size but by its atmosphere. Even the entrance through the glass pyramid is an experience in itself: modern and iconic at the same time.

The collection is overwhelming. From the Mona Lisa to the Venus of Milo to masterpieces from Egypt, Mesopotamia and the Islamic world, the Louvre offers a unique overview of the art history of humanity. The variety is particularly impressive: Ancient, Renaissance, Baroque, Modern – all under one roof.

Brilliant Art. This is a must visit when going to Paris. We went early and the queues were pretty big. The place is huge. The paintings were amazing. After a few hours it was really busy and my son felt quite overwhelmed by the amount of people there. It was a brilliant experience and the audio guide was very helpful. Sadly did not get to see everything in there.
Lovely day out. Lovely day out, arrive in plenty of time as large queues. Lots of photo opportunities. Lots to see and the place was immaculate. Lots of staff and toilet facilities.

Infinite museum. A boundless museum full of works of art of all kinds, including the sculpture Love and Psyche by Canova, The Mona Lisa and the Venus of the Rocks by Leonardo, but the quantity and quality of the works on display is boundless. In addition, the museum is a sum of buildings with some rooms of considerable beauty. So many crowds, but having a plan of what to see you can manage the time. Inevitable in Paris.

Amazing Experience, Even For Those Who Consider Themselves Not Into Art. The Louvre is a must do when visiting Paris. I don’t consider myself an art lover or knowledgeable about art, however found this experience very enjoyable, and it gave me a greater appreciation. I loved it.

I would highly recommend getting your tickets ahead of time to avoid the lineups. I recommend going early so you can capture some photos outside before the hoards of tourists show up. If you wait until after your tour, the property will be packed, making decent photos challenging.

I also highly recommend booking a small group tour with a guide to hit the highlights of the Louvre. It would take you many days to go through the entire property, however with a guide you can hit the highlights pretty efficiently.

This is an extremely busy place with an incredible amount of tourists, so pack some patience.

It is worth your time to visit the Louvre when spending some time in Paris.

Nice.. The Louvre is an absolutely impressive place — the richness of the collections, the variety of works (from antiquity to modern art), the majestic architecture, all this makes it a must for anyone visiting Paris. You’ll be amazed at the Mona Lisa, the Venus de Milo, the Egyptian halls or the decorative arts department; the Royal Palace Mood adds an almost magical dimension to the visit.

MUST HAVE!. We had already visited this museum on three other occasions.

Everything is wonderful, even the pavilions and rooms.

It's worth it anyway.

Overwhelmingly great collection. To point out the obvious, the Louvre is unbelievably huge. I would recommend having a route in mind and knowing what you want to see in advance, to make sure you’re getting to what you want. Louvre - Pablo Daniel Moyano Ilundain. The Louvre Museum in Paris is the most visited art museum in the world and a universal cultural icon. An ancient royal palace, it houses more than 35,000 works, including the Mona Lisa, the Venus de Milo and the Victory of Samothrace. Its blend of classic architecture and the modern glass pyramid of I. M. Pei makes it a symbol of the union between tradition and innovation.

Beautiful. We bought our tickets on their website. We were able to get in pretty quickly. Beautiful museum. Make sure to go during the night to take pictures outside because the lights make it incredible. The Mona Lisa painting is the most crowded area in the museum. Otherwise it’s not crowded and you can easily spend the whole day looking at masterpieces.

It was too wide and I was lost.. Join a priority admission tour, going straight to the Mona Lisa. We walked past Venus and Mike to the Mona Lisa, and then went around freely. There are always crowds in front of famous paintings, so you can't miss anything in a textbook. But it was really big, so it was hard to find a toilet. It was also difficult to understand the movement between buildings called wings. I'm exhausted from walking around in the wide and confusing.

I felt like a half-day visit was not enough, and I felt like I had had enough.

I think it’s a must see once. If you’re coming for Paris for the first time, I think you have to get some tickets for the Louvre you will need to get them in advance with the dedicated entry point if you’re planning on doing more than one museum, I was looking into the museum pass

Not just Mona Lisa. The Louvre is excellent but gets so busy mainly for Mona Lisa. Try to explore the other wings for a bit of peace and the Richelin cafe is a good place to relax also. A must for a Paris trip but expect it to be busy

Security of the museum. I visited the Louvre Museum and the Musée d'Orsay last week and left with mixed feelings. Both museums are part of humanity's cultural heritage and house priceless works of art, yet the interior security is practically nonexistent.

In most rooms there are no guards present, although you can see empty chairs where they should be stationed. The few that are there pay no attention whatsoever, spending the entire time looking at their phones.

The most concerning moment happened at the Musée d'Orsay: a visitor touched a work of art in front of a room full of people and no one reacted. I had to be the one to tell him not to do it.

This stands in stark contrast to other world-class museums such as the MoMA in New York, where security is real and active.

Given that the jewelry heist at the Louvre took place in October 2025 and the stolen pieces have still not been recovered, it is surprising that to this day no visible changes have been made to everyday security protocols. Any attentive visitor can notice it.

I hope this experience serves as a wake-up call for the responsible authorities to take concrete action and protect a heritage that belongs to all of humanity.

Amazing. A must see in Paris. The architecture itself is beautiful. The museum of course has amazing art. Honestly, a little overwhelming. So much to see, impossible to see in just one trip. Of course you get saturated after a while. We ended up staying for about 3 hours with our guide, which I think is also a must, otherwise you just end up wandering around a lot

Art all over the place. We didn't bought the tickets at the official museum site, because when you search for it there is a lot of different sites selling and it front image is made to cheat you. We bought it through a site that uses My Top Tour App and we paid €51 each, when the official ticket for europeans citizens is €22 only. We went on 2nd June when the Museum opened at 10:30 only, what created a giant queue. The way as Mona Lisa is presented doesn't allow to see the pictures on the side walls and it is a pretty. But is difficult to find another place in the world with so many art to be seen. The staff is helpfull and is always available.
Never.. It is a beautiful museum, but it is a disaster; there is no staff for information, not even friendly ones.

Kind of crowded, but worth it to see the art. We are a family and we have mixed opinions on this. Our child is writing some of the review, and I (his mom) am writing another part of the review.

My Child's review:

It was very crowded. If you are looking for the absolute best museum in Paris, I wouldn't recommend the Louvre Museum unless you love, or are OK, with crowds. You should go early in the morning, as it could be very crowded later.

My review:

As an art lover, I have been lucky to visit the Louvre several times. The incredible art never ceases to amaze me. However, the size and crowds can be overwhelming at times. So be sure to go find the most famous pieces, like the Mona Lisa, first. Regardless, it is a museum not to be missed if you are in Paris!

Not As Good As The Vatican Museum. The Louvre was once a medieval fortress until Napoleon and other high ranking nobles built onto it. It houses over 35,000 works of art. You will never see its entirety in one visit. Apparently a lot of those old classic masters had a rebellious streak and French kings were able to pick up the art cheap because the paintings didn’t fit accepted norms and were unsellable in Italy.

The crowds there were impressive. Our guide got us priority access to the Mona Lisa which we greatly appreciated, otherwise we would have had to wait behind 100s of people to catch a glimpse. We didn’t take a lot of pictures here. It was impressive and even though only saw a tiny bit of it, I was much more impressed with the rooms at The Vatican.

To do!. Huge! Once inside, you don’t know where to go. The place is clean and to visit. Plan almost the day to do everything.

I think the plan is wrong we bcp turned in circles.

Point of disappointment: the staff: unprofessional I caught many staff either bored sitting on their chair or chatting with their colleague as if they were on coffee break: scandalous. I even saw the annoyance of one of them at the entrance because a visitor could not scan her ticket. Another shouted at a visitor because he was too close, he did not even move, he shouted from his chair.

Finally, toilets: dirty, no soap.

Visit of the Louvre in January 2026: very good experience.. Fewer people than in high season, which makes it easier to move around and enjoy the works in good conditions. The atmosphere is pleasant and quieter.

The only downside is that well-known areas like the Mona Lisa remain crowded.

Tip: come early or late in the day and book in advance.

In short, an ideal time to visit the Louvre quietly.
An appreciable guided tour. A pleasant and guided discovery! A guide on the theme of the great works of the Louvre evoked with simplicity -

Michel Angel / Robin - Living Body. The face-to-face “Michelangelo/Rodin” turns into a formidable confrontation of the works of the 19th century sculptor with his michelangelesque sources, because Rodin very often analyzed the sculptures and paintings of the genius of the Renaissance.

The exhibition brings together marble, bronze, plaster, terracotta, castings and a very rich graphic production. The course emphasizes the formal and conceptual issues that lead to the same ambition: to make visible the inner energy of the body.

By showing filiations, borrowings and diversions, the exhibition invites us to rethink sculpture as a laboratory for artistic innovations. The masterpieces are matched by works inspired by Michelangelo.

The downside of this exhibition, the few sculptures by Michelangelo in this exhibition.
Amazing. It has always been my preferred location to observe historical events.

Older art in old king's palace. The museum is huge and it takes a lot of time to see everything or should I say as much as possible. The signage is not the best and is really easy to miss something. Limited numbers of bathrooms are also a minus. Fortunately there was not too many people and we were able to see a lot.

Easy to get lost in this massive place. But so beautiful and of course Mona Lisa.. This place is huge and not easy to navigate. Lots of up and down stairs. Make sure your knees are ready. very crowded even with a timed entry. But to see the Mona Lisa and other one-of-a-kind art is worth the effort. Plan on a long day to see as much as you can. But it would really take several days to see it all.

Worth ging once.... Somehow a must-go.

The Pyramide is impressing, some Art pieces as well.

But definately not one of the best museums in Europe.
You need a few days. Very crowded. Very difficult to navigate. Great facilities for those who have limited nobility. Helpful staff. A must do experience

Very improveable queue theme for everything. I recommend getting up early and booking early and still going early. Monday 30/03/26 we had reservation at 10:00 to enter at 9:30 we were already there and took almost 2 hours to enter. Better to enter at the top, the rest of the entrances are very crowded. Smooth cute theme could be improved with a zigzag row and people move not as is now that they are all crowded and save themselves who can, bad or very bad organization in that aspect.

Come on, you realize it's been two hours between the queue to get in and the queue as a woman to go to the bathroom.

If you have this to book, you enjoy the museum very much.

Do yourself a favor and take a guided tour with skip-the-line tickets. This place is MASSIVE and since I had a limited, probably once-in-a-lifetime, stay in Paris, I didn't want to waste time or save a few bucks trying to do this on my own. I'm so glad I got a guided, skip-the-line tour and was inside within 10 minutes at opening. We even took our time looking at and talking about the art along the way; it was probably an hour in before we saw Mona Lisa, yet there still wasn't much of a crowd. Her eyes will follow you wherever you are in the room, so don't worry about being dead-center for a photo with her - I stood off to the side and it looks like she's looking at my camera. I learned a lot about the art I was seeing, (that other people were rushing right by obliviously), and I was amazed at everything! We only had 2 or 3 hours on the tour, and as we finished up, it was ridiculously crowded. The huge rooms we'd been in earlier in the day, were packed and it took me 45 mintues just to get back out of there on my own. As I left, the lines to get in and get through security stretched for a LONG time, and I shuddered to think how long it would take those people to get inside. There are eating places inside (I wanted and tried to find Angelina's, but couldn't), but the one cafe I did see had a huge line so I'd also recommend not counting on eating here, but find something else.

For me, it was worth visiting, as I wanted to see it and to see some of the famous works of art inside. But I recommend paying extra for a guided tour - you'll get so much more out of it, it will be more enjoyable for you, and you'll have the rest of your day to either brave the crowds on your own, or visit something else.

Unfortunately, this is a problem too, because there is too much to see. I started my visit around noon and six hours later (without having taken a break) I had not yet finished... I left without seeing at least 10% of the museum.

Truly noteworthy to be in the largest museum in the world!. We tried to pick out a "top ten" route that would show a variety of the works on a somewhat chronological and wing order. The Venus wing was nice, the Mona Lisa room very crowded and we completely overshot Liberty Leading the People and Napoleon crowning himself. We had to back track and got the obligatory photos. From the grand daughter's perspective, many of the paintings were dark and dealt with death-it was probably too early to introduce them to some of those topics. The free lockers were centrally located and appreciated, as was the selection for gifts. A highlight was to find the Spot It/Dobble game in the version of the Louvre. Of course we bought it and we were pleasantly surprised to see that the children knew many of the characters on the cards!

Must-see. It is Obviously a must; the richness of the place, the history and the architecture is splendid.

On the other hand, displaying a clear circuit, having attentive staff and interested in guiding us in this maze, would be appreciated.

Louvre Paris. We were able to see the Mona Lisa with a sparkle ✅ Even though we had a ticket at 9:30 at the entrance, they opened the door at 11:30 for a meeting. Excellent little to improve. All very well and has many categories but I find it somewhat expensive their tickets as they should be for students from inside and outside the country should be half

our tour guide was very informative with lots of fun facts...I will note that she stated many times that she did not like the English or Americans (wasn't sure if she was kidding or not)

lots to see so wear comfy shoes an after the tour, plan to just getting lost in the art and the beauty!

and yes, the Mona Lisa is very small!

Extraordinary museum but very crowded and little organized. We visited the Louvre Museum with reservation at 16:00. Despite the booked time, we still had to wait in line at the entrance, probably because it was a busy day.

The museum is amazing and the works are just amazing. However, once inside, we found the rooms very chaotic and full of people, making it difficult at times to enjoy the works calmly.

However, it remains a must for those coming to Paris, but we recommend to organize well before entering and, if possible, choose less crowded times.

An essential step. Always spectacular, every time is a fascinating experience. If you can enter at a time with less turnout, the visit is really enjoyable; otherwise it becomes quite chaotic and tiring.

The museum is very large and dispersive, but this is part of the experience and its grandeur.

The only negative note: 4 euros for a drink seems an excessive price. I understand the context, but it gives a bit of an impression of a disproportionate increase.

History. The Louvre Museum is a truly wonderful place. Touring its halls is like traveling through history, surrounded by priceless works of art and impressive cultural heritage. The collection is extensive and diverse, and each visit offers the opportunity to discover something new and fascinating.

However, I must point out that my experience with some museum workers was not so positive. Staff unkind or unwilling to answer questions, and some seemed irritated or confrontational. In addition, during my visit they closed an entire floor of the museum without providing any explanation; I asked about it, but I got no answer, and there were still several hours before the museum closed. This type of situation can be frustrating for visitors and somewhat overshadows the experience.

Despite this, I highly recommend visiting the Louvre for its art, history and unique cultural experience, hoping that public attention and communication can improve.

To do well that the organization is reviewing. The museum is beautiful and super large, so big that it is difficult to orientate, not all rooms are numbered. It's a hell of a mess. The principle of a definitive exit through the pyramid is absurd (actually 1 exit is allowed to eat at lunch) you would save a lot of time to spend there to get from one walkway to another.

Something else people are sometimes very uncivil by allowing themselves to touch works... I've had to police a few times and I've been looked at like I'm wrong!

Otherwise you have to plan time, it is impossible to do everything in a day and you come out very tired.

Residence of Kings. The Louvre is the Louvre and that says it all. The history, the art, the luxury of life of kings is just impressive! Not impressed with the organization of work for a comfortable visit to the museum .

Opens under the snow. I went to the Louvre with a friend under the snow, there was a slight delay but nothing mind-boggling.

Magnificent museum by its works, some rooms have very explanatory cartels and makes us want to learn even more about these exceptional works, besides rooms much less provided in explanation.

The museum had quite a lot of people but since it was visiting outside the school holidays it was largely possible to circulate there. The beauty of the Louvre under the snow and moreover at night made the moment magical.

I would still say that the Mona Lisa room was disappointing, not especially for the painting itself but more because I would have liked to see the works around it but it was practically impossible outside of the wedding of Cana facing it. I advise you to eat under the pyramid if you can. Lockers are free and are below the pyramid behind one of the information counters.

Also plan your visit as some rooms are often closed due to renovations and you may be disappointed.

Amazing Museum. The museum is amazing. The paintings etc are just overwhelming with so many masterpieces. The only issue is the number of people going to the museum just ruins it. There should be limits on the numbers each day. The other thing that amazed me were people rushing past masterpieces to see the Mona Lisa. Yes it’s an amazing painting but what about taking the time to look at the other ones?
Good but not suitable for children. A visit where everyone can admire works that they like. Too bad there isn’t a children’s route or something fun like there is in many other museums...

The Louve. The Louve is just as impressive as imagined and its size can be overwhelming. Go with a plan and good map. The Louve galleries could be better identified and directional signage could be improved to make the maps more useful. Exhibits could be better labeled to take further advantage of the audio devices. Overall, however, a great experience.

Lots to possibly see. Very busy, lots of things to see. Probably best to allow 3 hours before you get the desire to stop looking at pots from many years ago. Tick the main items off the bucket list and that is probably ok for the first visit. Wonder at why some countries are not asking for their historical artefacts back. Impressive building which is often lost as there is just too much to see!

Worth the visit when in Paris.. This is a cool museum. Worth purchasing tickets online before and then queuing in line at your chosen time selected.

The restaurant has a cool entrance via the glass pyramid with escalators taking you down underground. From there you can go to different sections of the museum.

Obviously the busiest part was the Mona Lisa which is cool to see. I would recommend seeing this when you come to Paris.

Majestic. One of the most beautiful museums in the world, with iconic works that really leave you breathless. One above all, Leonardo's Mona Lisa, the most famous painting in the world! For me, second only to the Uffizi!

Must have experienced!. We had booked tickets with which you had access in a certain time window in Museeum. Faster, easier admission. There are many exciting things to discover. It is best to get a map of the museum at the infoschallter so that you have an overview which artworks are located where!

Plan your visit ahead!. We bought our tickets online through ticket.louvre.fr and arrived 15 minutes before the indicated entrance time, only to discover several long queues forming around the Pyramid. Our queue was already about 100 meters long and eventually stretched to nearly 150 meters, as many visitors had arrived an hour or more before their scheduled entry. Standing outside in severe weather conditions is far from pleasant, and it can be frustrating to wait so long before even getting inside the museum.

The Louvre holds an overwhelming number of masterpieces and fine artworks, and it is nearly impossible to explore all three main wings in a single visit:

• Denon Wing – Mona Lisa, French Crown Jewels, Greek Antiquities

• Richelieu Wing – Near Eastern Antiquities (5000 BC), Decorative Arts, Northern European paintings (1350–1850)

• Sully Wing – History of the Louvre, Egyptian Antiquities, and more

The official website provides clear information about which galleries are open or partly closed, and you can download the museum map in advance.

We spent almost five hours inside and were eventually asked to leave because of closing time. For this reason, I strongly advise planning your visit ahead of time—identify the masterpieces you want to see through the official website rather than relying solely on audio guides to direct you from room to room.

Food is another point to consider. If you get hungry, your choices are limited to the museum’s own (and quite expensive) cafés or going down to the entrance area beneath the Pyramid, which is essentially near the exit. Eating your own food inside the museum is not allowed—not even in resting areas with chairs and no artworks around.

The Louvre is closed on Tuesdays. On Wednesdays and Fridays, it stays open later, until 9 p.m.; on all other days, the museum closes around 6 p.m., and galleries begin clearing out at about 5:30 p.m. If you don’t plan your route properly, you may not even have time to take photos of the artworks before staff begin guiding visitors out.

Visiting the Louvre is an unforgettable experience, but thoughtful planning can make the difference between a stressful and a truly enriching day.
Essential. Huge in every way. If you go to Paris you should visit it preparing good footwear and patience. It's fabulous. A visit is not enough.

Plan to book tickets online!. FORTUNATELY we booked tickets online (free for students under 25, by the way, which is not insignificant).

Pros (outside of collections):

-effective dressing room

\- toilet (provide a queue for the ladies)

\- snack/bakery/pastry available and various beverage points (sometimes overpriced)

Finding the Mona Lisa is sometimes a feat for those who do not read a plan correctly but, fortunately, escalators and elevators make the task less painful.

Only negative point:

People who come to the museum to finish their nose on... their cell phones...

Your baby and the Louvre. LOUVRE visit with a BABY. The elevator in the pyramid was a nice greeting, yet inside the museum getting around with a stroller was a real challenge. Elevators were not well marked.

Families with babies need a recommended walking route.

The Monna Lisa needs its own special amphitheater for all the attention it is getting.

One final point, gift Shop has very few items. MERCI PARIS

To many people. The museum in general was great to see from inside and outside it was not so great to see the closed segments because of the robbery a few weeks ago. But it was very crowded and I couldn't walk a meter without bumping in to someone.

Overwhelming and mind-blowing. Sister and I had booked tickets online but that doesn't mean you walk in immediately. Still lots of lineups and security to go through (this was not long after the jewelry heist). Lineup to go to the washroom that was 20 minutes long. Cloakroom had available lockers at no charge. Pro tip: Take a picture of your locker and environs to remind you where your stuff is parked. No water/drink bottles, or long umbrellas allowed, and if you carry a Swiss army knife or sharp objects, remove before heading to the Louvre.

We wanted to see the jewelry gallery, but that was still closed. We wandered through Greek and Roman galleries, and found there were plenty of seats to sit down in. The one thing I really recommend is the Napoleon III apartments in Richelieu wing. Like being on a PBS show with over-the-top gilt and gloriousness! You should go with a plan for what you want to see, as it is really overwhelmingly huge. We didn't even bother with Mona Lisa bc it was very people-y and we were running out of people steam. Nice gift shop with a selection of museum memorabilia. Past the gift shop is an area with Laduree, chocolate, snacks, and it leads to the subway. Gorgeous museum full of humankind's greatest treasures. And btw, I love that glass pyramid; it shouldn't work aesthetically, but it does.

Immense. Certainly it is the museum par excellence. Famous paintings, sculptures, art of all kinds. Immense, to miss the day. You must of course be passionate, I who am not after two hours I could have already gone out, once I saw the finest pieces. Be prepared for queues especially for the Mona Lisa, and groups with guides that clog up the "traffic". Another tip: if you have the entrance at a certain time, queue at least half an hour before, otherwise enter half an hour later, the time booked.
A Must. The best world museum with alot to see and learn.This is a must see.Very crowded during the day.Very stunning. I love art.a great place with different art collection

Stunning museum but the price of food is extortionate. We attended here on our last day in Paris and I had forgotten how huge this place is. Even if you are not a fan of art, the architecture is stunning. The jewels were closed when we visited (a few days after the robbery).

Queuing for your booked time is outside around the pyramids with no shelter. You can also queue undercover through the shopping area but when we left the museum this queue was incredibly long. We spent around 2-3 hours here.

I would recommend getting tickets prior to attending as there were quite a few disappointed people. It was very busy but there was still plenty of room to get good views of the art on display.

The price to get into the museum I thought was reasonable, however the price of lunch was extortionate. Had I realised I would have certainly bought got food outside before entering. We paid £41 for 2 sandwiches, 3 crisps, 3 drinks and a piece of cake!

Take a time travel adhv art from all over the world. Enjoy art from all over the world and (almost) all eras

Select the items you want to view in advance

Take the time to discover the museum building as well
Paris. Very good experience in Louvre in the heart of the city of Paris. Very good place to take photos it's good....😄

One to tick off the list. A must see in Paris. We booked for an 1830 slot on a Friday evening but were allowed entry around 1800. Security upon entering was very lax - considering it was five days since the robbery you would think they would be hot on checking you thoroughly.

The Mona Lisa room is bedlam. Most people are polite and let everyone get their turn at the front.

The map they give you is really difficult to read and locate what you want to see. There’s also barely any signage inside aside from directions to the most famous items. We spent two hours inside and barely scratched the surface.

I would definitely recommend the later slot if your schedule allows. We were in the adjoining shopping centre on Sunday morning and the queues to enter were never ending.

Half the ladies toilets were out of service which led to long queues.

If you have a teen with you and they look older than they are, make sure you take a photo of their ID to prove their age.

Amazing!. A must see for your first time in Paris but you have to get there for 9am and plan your day out properly. The Denon wing is the busiest - by 10:15 the entire wing was absolutely gridlocked with people so plan to have Denon out and done by 12 at the latest! A side note - be prepared for instagram style photoshoots. I am not adverse to a few photos myself but the amount of people going full photographer in front of works of art was insane and indicative of the individualistic consumerism that permeates popular tourist attractions - there were similar issues (arguably on a bigger scale) at the Opera. Nonetheless, the Richelieu wing and especially the Sully wing (we went to see Egyptian artifacts specifically) were not as busy. Similarily, the Napoleon apartments were almost empty at 10:30am - a great experience. However, by midday, it was jam packed. Something to note is we could only find one cafe inside the actual museum - in the Denon wing near the mona lisa and European art. All the other cafes were after you exited into the Carousel / under the pyramid - so we couldn’t take a break midway through. So, if you’re planning to do a lot, try to start from the other end of the Denon wing, eat at the cafe after, then continue. It took us six hours to do Denon & most of Sully - and that was only on Level 1 and 0! You can’t see it all in one day.

Get ready to walk, lots of walking!. A very interesting attraction, lots of history is on display. I would recommend a visit but be ready to walk, staircases everywhere you travel in the museum. The only item I was surprised with was the size of the Mona Lisa, Large display for a very small painting.

Breathtaking works of art. The famous Mona Lisa is the main highlight of this famous museum, but you definitely need HOURS to visit the whole place.

Works of art are fabulous.

Unfortunately the gem exhibit or section of museum is closed i guess because of recent theft.

Tons of people visiting on this day (Friday) & marble steps.

Definitely has changed since my 1st & last visit (more than 20 years ago),but still impressive but appears larger.

If you love art this is a must see.

The glass pyramids (now the main entrance) are very cool.

Excellent. World class museum in the heart of Paris with a lot to see and learn.Good history.A must visit and see in Paris.

Very crowded but still worth to see.

To do. A must in Paris. Remember to book the ticket in advance and consult the open and closed rooms according to the days of visit. This can be a little frustrating. It is regrettable that the entire museum is not fully open every day. Enjoy visiting it at the end of the day, you will have easier access to some very popular works.

known to all!. If you do not have the time or patience to wait in queues to enter the Museum, you can navigate the surrounding area. The buildings are by themselves impressive and imposing. Tourists are photographed in the most comical stations holding the top of the pyramid, feeding pigeons or following guides. In the area are parked and three-wheeled coco taxi for those who wish. On one side in the perimeter of the Louvre is the glass pyramid with the descent inside the museum and opposite the arch with the Napoleonic triumphs. Beyond the arch you will also encounter retailers, obviously they are not allowed to get closer to the perimeter of the Museum. Near the pyramid is the statue of Louis XIV, which was erected in 1988.

A satisfying visit - despite a partial strike. The experience itself in the Louvre was great.

This is not a museum that can be covered in one day (at least we can't) - so it is better to visit multiple time - each time focusing on one part of the museum.

This time we focused on Italian and French painters. Not a picture to miss (and this is not just about the Mona Lisa - there is plenty to see other than this painting).

We also wanted to look at British and Dutch painting - but due to a strike - this floor was closed (hence the reduced rate to 4 instead of 5) - this was really a disappointment.

My advice - don't make it too long (4 or 5 hours per visit) and concentrate on a certain wing/topic.

If you don't have enough knowledge on a topic - perhaps consider a guide tour (we didn't...)

Don’t just go for the Mona Lisa. Very busy and a long queue - make sure you book in advance! The crowd to see the Mona Lisa was overwhelming (to be expected). We particularly enjoyed the medieval Louvre and gasped when we saw Napoleon III’s apartments.

Crowds Ruin the Beauty. We booked guided tour to visit. We were given immediate access (no waiting in line) and shown Winged Victory, Mona Lisa and Venus. We were also given information about the palace and what has been recently unearthed (previous structure hundreds of years ago) that was interesting. The museum is beautiful but too crowded. Almost impossible to enjoy, especially when going to see Mona Lisa. There is no order to the crowd—-people shove their way to the front of the masses to see. I wish we had more

time to see more but we wanted to escape the massive crowds.

Beautiful but dispersive. A real maze in which it is easy to get lost. But it is worth it when you find yourself in front of immeasurable masterpieces.

The stop is a must when staying in Paris.

Beautiful, but slightly chaotic. The Louvre is undoubtedly one of the most beautiful and richest museums in the world: the works on display are extraordinary and are definitely worth a visit. However, the overall experience was a bit chaotic. The spaces are very crowded and the management of visitor flows leaves something to be desired. Even the directions inside are not always clear, and often you end up going around for a long time before finding the rooms you are looking for.

I still recommend the visit — because it is worth it — but it would be nice to see a better organization and more intuitive signage, to fully enjoy this unique place.

Review of the Louvre Museum – Paris, France. The Louvre is everything you imagine and more - The collection is enormous and beautifully curated, showcasing everything from ancient Egyptian artifacts to Renaissance masterpieces. Seeing the Mona Lisa in person is, of course, a highlight, but the museum offers so much more. It’s best to plan ahead — buy your tickets online and go early to avoid the biggest crowds. You could easily spend an entire day here, but even a few hours will give you an unforgettable experience.

A magical visit to the Louvre. Visiting the Louvre was an amazing experience. It feels like I could spend days exploring and still discover something new. I loved the mix of modern art and the classical exhibitions. In particular, the glass pyramid at the entrance adds a contemporary touch that contrasts beautifully with the museum’s historic architecture and art. Of course, everyone comes to see the Mona Lisa, however, I found it a bit overrated... The painting is quite small, and there’s always a large queue in front of it. Even so, I would definitely recommend visiting the Louvre for its incredible atmosphere. Everything about the museum is picturesque: the architecture, the art, and its stunning location.

Beautiful but crazy busy. If you decide to go to the Louvre go early. I got the earliest ticket time at 9:00 am. I entered in through the Carousel entrance in the mall. A lot less people enter the Louvre through this entrance. When you get in the museum get a map first thing. Then I went straight to the Mona Lisa. I had a list of about 15 things I wanted to see. Spent about 2 1/2 hours in there and then by 11:30 it was too crowded with people. I had good experience but go into the Louvre with a plan.

Great visit but some areas could be controlled better. Really beautiful museum, well laid out and helpful maps where given which was good for finding your way around such a big space. However, the viewing of the Mona Lisa needs to be regulated as it was just a free for all which came with a lot of pushing and shoving whilst you try to make your way to the front to see the painting. Loads of staff there but nothing was being regulated to help with that experience. Other than that, experience was great!

choose some late hours with reserved tickets and pick up the must-see rooms. Great museum, but this was the second time we visited after a couple of years, and almost the whole 2 level was closed again. Also, at least 3/4 of the toilets were out of order, which caused some frustration with some thousands of visitors inside. Again, choose some late hours with reserved tickets and pick up the must-see rooms. You are not able to visit and see everything anyway in one day. Late hours are free to make a reservation just days before, and then there's very few visitors compared to midday.
Not just the jolly.... museum very large and with different environments and objects, maybe a little too full to be able to see as much as possible in a single day
A must. You will not be disappointed,booking time slots saves a lot of queuing . Staff are amazing at keeping the line moving . If you like art this is the place for you .

An incredible museum. The Louvre is huge and impressive. Just the entrance with the pyramid, it makes you want. Inside, you travel through history: Egyptian mummies, Greek statues, great Renaissance paintings... The most striking remains the Mona Lisa and Victory of Samothrace, but there is so much to see that you can’t do everything in one visit. It’s beautiful, fascinating, but also a bit tiring because it’s so big.

The Louvre Museum, a journey through art and history. Visiting the Louvre Museum was a fascinating experience. From the moment we arrived, the iconic glass pyramid made us feel as if we were entering a unique place in the world. The museum is enormous, and each room amazes with masterpieces spanning from antiquity to modernity.

My family and I toured some of its most famous treasures: the Mona Lisa, the Venus de Milo, and the Victory of Samothrace. Although it was quite crowded, the excitement of seeing these pieces in person was indescribable. We also enjoyed less crowded rooms, where we could calmly appreciate paintings, sculptures, and objects steeped in history.

The best part was sharing everything, discussing every detail, and marveling at every corner. It's a museum that demands time and energy, but every minute is worth it. The Louvre is not just a museum; it's a must-see cultural experience for anyone visiting Paris.

A must. Undoubtedly, a magnificent museum.

The problem is the number of visitors. There are so many people that works such as The Mona Lisa, the Raft of the Jellyfish or the Venus of Milo are impossible to approach.

Perhaps it would be useful to find a system to allow only X people at a time.

GREAT EXPERIENCE. Unfortunately our reservation fell on the day of the demonstrations on 10/9. Visit very disturbed because the opening of the museum was very delayed. Few personnel to inform us. We had to come back in the afternoon for a reserved tour for the morning, positive: no waiting to enter and a very smooth tour. Of course the museum is beautiful, I think it is better to come in winter to have less people.

Mixed. Superb museum, seen for the 2nd time, but so much to see.

A really bad toilet rating, we don't go for it, but sometimes it can be useful.

Some closed, others bite-sized and overflowing...and others with crazy queues.

I know the state of the toilet depends on the people who use it, but what an image for outsiders, this museum is a showcase for our country, so a little more cleaning would be welcome.

Overwhelming and stifling heat, no air conditioning, very unpleasant.

Good visit with kids. I've been to the Louvre many times, but this was my first visit with kids. We got advance, timed tickets, which was helpful. It was still very crowded, especially in the most popular areas, but was manageable.

Moany Lisa. This was my 4th time in Paris and had to visit the Louvre this time. However I booked my ticket from a company called Walker tours. Do not purchase ANYTHING from this company. They are a scam and resell the same ticket numerous times and charge you triple. I managed to contact the real official site and they confirmed this however I still got to visit without incident for my pre booked time slot. I’m not an art buff but seeing some famous art was worth it. The bottle neck queue for the Mona Lisa was irritating and very warm due to the amount of people but again worth it to say I saw the Mona Lisa. Metro was excellent mode of transport to get there from where I was staying just off the Arc De Triomphe. Be wary if Google tickets as the very first one was the scammers and not the official Louvre website. A lovely experience that was not marred by the scammers.
Stunning. Visited the louvre with a group of friends definitely worth a visit if in paris. rooms and paintings are stunning although it can be busy there are so many different rooms which are quieter

Full of the world's best artwork. The Louvre Museum is truly remarkable - from Egyptian Antiquities to Greek, Roman and Islamic Arts - there are great collections in every category. The biggest attraction, of course, is The Mona Lisa, although the crowd was so large that it's hard to enjoy the quiet from up close. Still, I was impressed by the Napoleon III Apartments and the Victory of Samothrace. It is a dream place for those who love art, but it is impossible to visit everything in one day.
Mona Lisa. The main attraction is too far away to be viewed properly. There is far too much scrimmaging for anyone to enjoy it. Lots of other things to explore. Restaurant is cheap & good.

Best museum I've ever visited. Beautiful, great, rooms, paintings, sculptures, spectacular artifacts. But for me I went by car, expensive parking and pretty big queues to the entrance, waiting for the rain (in August).

Immense and dispersive. This royal residence became a museum in 1793 and houses works from the 7th millennium BC until the mid-19th century. The collections are displayed on five levels and in three interconnecting wings bearing the names of three illustrious French figures: Sully, Richelieu and Denon. Seeing it all in a single day is virtually impossible, so I recommend choosing only the sectors that interest you. At the entrance there is the possibility to take the map in the language of your choice.

Bucket list this. Everyone should do the Louvre at least once.. The Louvre is always a good idea.

I booked my ticket a month in advance, and maybe that’s why everything felt so seamless—even the rain didn’t matter. I entered through a quieter side entrance, skipping the chaos at the main gate, and for a moment, it felt like the museum opened just for me.

Yes, it was crowded—tourists everywhere—and no, I didn’t see the Mona Lisa up close. I stood about 20 feet back, zoomed in with my camera, and took it in from afar. Too. Many. Tourists. !!!!! And still… it was enough.

Because the real magic of the Louvre is not in one painting—it’s in the endless corridors of history, light, shadow, and silence between brushstrokes. It’s the quiet awe that settles in your chest as you move from sculpture to canvas, room to room, century to century.

I spent four hours inside and could have stayed four more. The Louvre doesn’t just house art—it holds time, memory, and wonder. It’s not just a museum; it’s a feeling. One that lingers.

Louvre Museum. The Museum is huge, even with the whole afternoon till evening there, it is not enough. Make sure you have a list of what is your priority “must see” art. The room numbers are hard to find and not much help inside. No wonder other groups who wanted to enjoy more have their tour guide. But at least we have seen Monalisa and Venus de Milo , top of our list. We went on a Thursday, they said it is not crowded but this depends on you, I find it crowded, too. You must give enough time to take pictures outside of the museum too. We went back on a Tuesday afternoon to have a good view of the building and better photos, since the Museum is closed on Tuesdays.

Overall, I am still satisfied but if you are not an art lover, it is okay just to visit the building and no need to buy tickets to enter.

Overrated. Museum externally very nice, a mixture of new and old with a touch of elegance even in the modern. Too bad I expected more inside, many rooms closed and paintings placed not too clearly even if the staff was ready to help. Too much publicity for a museum that could give more.

Wifi available.

Good, just very busy.. A great museum and definitely a bucket list item seeing pieces like the Mona Lisa in person. The museum is very expansive with themed rooms which found it easy to find what you were looking for, even though there was inadequate signposting at points. The only issue was that I found the experience to be overwhelming due to the amount of people, potentially implementing stricter entry time allocations would help with the issue.

Probably a must if you're in Paris.. It was a great visit, it is not without reason the most famous museum in the world. Beautiful! and all roads lead to Mona Lisa. It's really crowded there, but that was to be expected

The best museum in the world. Nothing to say as everything has already been said! One of the best museums in the world. The only flaw is the few places to eat a sandwich bought at the in-house boulangerie cafe

Wanted Monna Lisa!. First visit to the Louvre (incredible but true ...).

Metro/Carousel/museum, convenient. Tickets booked online for a reasonable wait (bag search required).

We definitely want to see her, the mythical woman. But it is still part of the fighter’s journey: a crazy world in the dedicated room, mass of tourists (including me) congregated, waiting for the selfies in the front row to stop to progress from millimeter to millimeter..., heat, sighs, patience, to end almost ejected on one side. The Lady deserves herself but does not enjoy! Too bad.

We come to our senses amid Egyptian antiquities that plunge me into my memories of the ancient and incredible Cairo Museum.

The Louvre, sublime place.

Must-see stop. You cannot visit Paris without making a stop at the Louvre, a must for lovers of art and history. The museum is vast, almost dispersive, and requires good organization to be appreciated at best. One of the aspects to be improved concerns the management of the influx around the most famous painting, The Mona Lisa: given the huge number of visitors, especially in high season, it would be appropriate to better regulate access to make the experience more enjoyable. The staff was courteous and helpful.

A lot of people and a few works less. I've been to the museum other times and in other years but I was impressed by the crowd circulating around the museum. More and more people seem to be interested in seeing Monalisa...

Its collection is unparalleled but I still missed some works that I remembered having seen such as the code of Hamurabi and the huge Assyrian sculptures of more than 5 meters high.

I think I could have a roadmap to facilitate access to the main works so that many tourists don't stay wandering through their hallways and obstructing access to some wings.

In any case, it will always be a lush experience to review your memorable works. And better yet outside the European summer holiday period.

Beautiful museum. The museum is really too much, surely one of the friendliest in the world. But the plan really sucks. Take inspiration from the Natural History Museum in New York and offer an app with real-time geolocation.

Staff at Cafe Mollien are unfriendly and unkind. The staff at the Café Mollien in the Louvre are unfortunately very unwelcoming and unkind. This spoils part of the experience in this museum, which is so exceptional. That’s a shame.

Unfortunately, the staff at Café Mollien in the Louvre are very unfriendly and unkind. It really spoils the overall experience of such a magnificent museum. It's truly disappointing.

Making time to enjoy yourself. I did enjoy the museum. We had a guide. This is a must. Way over crowds. Should have the tourist in at a time. Mona Lisa, well this was a let down. Again so many people crammed into a small room. And no one leaves.

Did I enjoy the time spent, Yes. Again, first time in, sign up with a guide.

Awesome but super crowded. Obviously the Louvre sets the standard for museums. But it is absurdly crowded. And I was told it gets worse. I cannot imagine it being more crowded. With so many people it’s super hot in almost every gallery. Ok enough of that. We had a guided tour. Where we just hit some of the biggies. Mona Lisa, Venus Di Milo, and such. Our guide, whose name I believe was Marie? Apologies, I do not remember. She was pretty good. Accent was a little thick at times. But she had lots of information and was able to be funny. Anyway, I’ve done it now, and don’t plan to face those crowds again.
Good Experience. One of the greatest museum in the world.Very well designed museum with alot of things to see and learn.We had a great day .worth a visit

Amazing Museum, Pack your Patience. We bought tickets online well in advance for the 9 am time slot.

(I would like to point out that there is a scam website that looks like the Louvre ticket office but it is not... beware of that because you cannot get your money back and they charge double the price.)

We arrived at 8:30 at the pyramid entrance, and there were a considerable number of people already in line with tickets. The line to buy tickets was insane. When the doors opened, we got right inside with little wait time.

We decided to see the Mona Lisa first to avoid the crowd, but, apparently so did everyone else. It was like a Filene's Basement sale in there. If you don't like crowds or people pushing and shoving you, or taking up space as they take 100 instagram photos of themselves, this is not the room for you.

Once we left that room, we found the majority of the museum to be less crowded, and it was relatively easy to see the pieces we wanted to see. We encountered a lot of crowds at the Venus de Milo as well. However, be aware that there are tour groups that will stand in front of a painting or sculpture, blocking it for others to see for an unreasonable amount of time. Additionally, many people are unaware that others exist in the world and may come over and stand right in front of you as you look at something. Pack your patience.

We gave ourselves four hours in the Louvre and saw a great deal in that time but honestly you can spend forever in there. We prepared a list ahead of time of the things we wanted to see. I had purchased a book online and we used that to make our list, but, the room numbers have been changed since that book was published. Please double check that if you use this method.

Amazing experience. There's a reason there are hours long queues to get in - it's literally the best museum in the world. We stayed from opening time to closing time. It was unbelievable. I would improve on the accessibility by making there be more water fountains as we were very hot.

Better wear comfortable shoes. Travelled as a family and was really looking forward to this spectacular place.

It's a huge building and contains impressive paintings and artefacts.

We visited in July and it was very crowded.

Even with the map supplied to us, we got incredibly lost and confused by all the different floor layouts and sections. There's also a huge amount of walking, and lifts are a priority for disabled only.

After 3 hours, we left having given up trying to find some paintings we really wanted to see.

French "hospitality" smudges an excellent museum. Imagine being so "big" you can't fail. Such is Musée du Louvre in 2026. Buy tickets at 9:00am to get a quiet look? Too bad, we'll open "sometime today" because we're understaffed. As a small business owner I know what call-outs look like and how it affects workflow, but for the largest art collection in Paris how many call outs do you have to have to be crippled? The fact that they completely refused to move pre-purchased tickets to another day led to unlimited entry around 2pm, by which time all major pieces looked like a mosh pit.

Too crowded!. Amazing culture but simply too packed. At times it felt like a shopping centre with poor signage. Why preserve such beautiful pieces for centuries and then provide such a naff customer experience? Less people, more systems would make this better.

Disappointment with ticket company. Careful!. Just buy your ticket from the official Louvre website. I bought 2 tickets to go with my fiancé for Get Your Guide, the day we couldn't find the Guide that would take us directly to the museum. We searched everywhere and found no one with a vest or company ID. The app of this company did not contain the tickets and when we were able to talk to them (after a Parisian helped us because the phone only accepts calls from local numbers) we were informed that they considered that we did not attend and did not provide us with our tickets. I think this company acted in bad faith, because if we were with the tickets we could have accessed the museum by the common line. I will never buy anything other than from the official website, I don't trust companies like this anymore. It remains my report of a terrible experience so that other people do not have the same disappointment we went through.

Didn't live up to the hype. If the Louvre didn't have the Mona Lisa it would be a very average museum in my opinion. The Venus de Milo and Winged Victory are very impressive. But the collection of antiquities pales in comparison to the British Museum in London. There is very little modern art if you enjoy that. The Mona Lisa room is so obnoxiously crowded you can't get within 20 yards of the painting. The painting of the Wedding at Cana on the wall opposite the Mona Lisa was honestly much more impressive. If you only have time for one art museum in Paris I would check out Musee D'Orsay instead, unless you just have to post a selfie in front of the Mona Lisa. The history of the Louvre's construction in the basement of the Sully wing, where you can go through the original moat with drawbridge supports, was quite interesting.

Chaotic and hard to navigate. The biggest museum in the world and don’t you know when you are inside. So hard to navigate, crowds of people and confusing directions. The staff are hit and miss as to whether they can help.

Massive queues for the ladies, huge queues to buy a drink. Queue again to see people looking at the Mona Lisa.

The building itself is stunning but perhaps best viewed from the outside.

Disorganised Chaos! Visit London instead!. I’ve no idea what was going on in the Louvre today - perhaps it was just a normal day? However, it felt like the museum was opening for the first time and didn’t have any staff or know how to operate a so-called state of the art establishment!

Anyway, we finally got in hours after our booked ticket time and saw the suggested list of treasures plus our own personal list of far superior treasures!

The Louvre building is very impressive and don’t miss Napoleon’s apartments in your rush to the crush to fleetingly see the Mona Lisa!

Do your research prior to your visit, the Louvre seems to change its room numbers periodically and the issued maps don’t bear much resemblance to the open floor plan. Know what you wish to see before you go, it will make your day more enjoyable. This visit could be a 5\* experience for your customers with a bit of effort!

The Louvre has a dull cataloguing system, they could make rooms far more impressive with a little imagination from the curators, without undermining the importance of the treasures.

Interestingly, we did not see a single visitor smiling or looking like they have spent their time well. Most looked totally bored and a few, we thought, had given up the will to live - I blame the dull curation!

Of course there are some beautiful things within that I have waited a long time to see, but frankly for a far superior art and treasure experience nothing beats the London Galleries and the British museum! In fact I could have flown to London in quicker time than the Louvre staff took to get their act together and open the doors today!

Come on France, you’re not a 3rd world country, inject a bit of 21st century ability into your outlook….also on a personal note, sell decaf coffee in the foodhall!

Not recommended. Despite pre-ordered tickets, I had to wait in line for a long time.

Despite the early hours, the museum was already very well attended, not to say overcrowded.

I could hardly watch the highlights of the Louvre, such as the Mona Lisa. It was a big crowd and as soon as you were in front of the painting, the staff urged you to move on. Overall, I could look at the painting for maybe 20 seconds, which was very disappointing for me.

I can therefore only recommend a visit to the museum to a limited extent.

After all, the entrance fee is not small

Poorly Managed. The Louvre was its usual awfully-managed self today.

Had tickets for 10:30 but it didn’t open on time and we finally got in at 12:00. Was there any message given out to the waiting queues? Ha, ha, of course not.

On arrival, saw 8 empty “Assistance” positions but no-one in any of them to provide assistance.

There are no indications on arrival in any Level as to what level you are on … you are supposed to know by osmosis.

Apart from in the Richelieu wing, most rooms don’t have a room number indicated in them, so whilst the visitor map is good, you can’t orient to where you are.

Very few ways to get up and down, so for example you get stuck in Egyptology when you really want to be up another level.

The sad thing is that these are all problems faced by other museums, except they aren’t so arrogant as to ignore any comments and think they are brilliant. Shame on you, Louvre management.

As to the visit, decide what you don’t like (eg Greek broken pottery) and don’t do any of that wing/floor. There are say 40 decent paintings and you can do all that you really need to see in 3 hours. By looking up paintings or artefacts you can see what room they are in, and plan accordingly.

The rules on security are all very woolly … but there are decent lockers and you can come back to them throughout the tour. Just be prepared to queue at the beginning of your visit and don’t expect everything to work properly or be communicated … they know best and you are a mere visitor.
This city is super dazzling to its mother. I found it original to see the museum in this form

A lot to see - plan in advance. There is a lot to see and wandering around gets very tiring. There were two Vermeer's, but mostly not really into the art displayed. Long queue outside despite arriving before the due time. Definitely worth visiting, but maybe plan what you want to see in advance.

Martin Schongauer exhibition. This small exhibition of works by Martin Schongauer will delight amateurs of Medieval art.

It contains mostly rather small engravings and religious paintings.

The highlight is an impressive painting of the Virgin

Must-see museum, management needs improvement!. Beautiful and fascinating museum, there is little to say! But the organization is terrible.. it takes at least 3 hours to visit, so do not set the last entry at 4:30 PM (last available tickets purchased days in advance) and start closing the sections from 5:30 PM, it is a joke! You force people to come up with "tricks" to enter earlier.. I advise you not to buy tickets after 3 PM!

AVOID THE QUEUE!. AVOID THE QUEUE!

We arrived at the entrance under the Pyramid at 1115 with tickets for 1130. There was a very long queue, and we were told it would take over an hour to get in.

An attendant at the back of this snaking queue advised us to go to the entrance PORTE DE LION as it would be quicker.

A sort walk took us there and there was a queue of about a dozen people and we were inside within FIVE MINUTES.

I hope this will help others to get round the museum’s mad ticket system. Why don’t they divide each time slot between the various entrances?

Once inside the porte de Lion you’ll have to search for the signs to show you the way to the rest of the museum.

Too busy, too expensive. So so busy, herded around like sheep, nowhere to sit, toilet queues horrendous, to be able to enjoy it you would be best to go at the quietest time, we were only in there a short time because we needed to get out and get some air, there must have been thousands of people in there, you have to get a timed slot so you would assume they only let so many in.

Underwhelming. We must be the exceptional couple that weren’t blown away by the Louvre. Yep, there’s a lot to see but if didn’t blow us away. The crowds are a turn off as well. Probably the least favourite of all the sights we saw in Paris

would not go again. While the louvre has some beautiful pieces, it is ruined by the amount of people they allow in. We were packed like sardines in really hot weather conditions, me and my mum were in for maybe 40 minutes if not less. I am glad to have gone but I wouldn't go again. They also do not accomodate to the amount of visitors well, there was a huge queue for the toilets at all times because they had barely any stalls at each stop. One only had two. It is absolutley ridiculous the amount of people that are allowed in there, such a shame because the place is so beautiful.

Impression. Instead of the visit time indicated on the ticket, we stood in the queue for 40 minutes.

The museum staff I had asked to show me the way to the Michelangelo gallery would not accompany me these few dozen meters, but merely pointed the direction with their hands.

The lighting is uneven in many halls, causing sun reflections to “dance” in the paintings.
Good but disappointed. Very disappointed on the armchairs not enough at the bar-restaurant and even in the museum for the elderly

Respect?. It’s funny that they’ve handed out signs asking for respect for workers when they’re the workers themselves who don’t respect you: it seems to bother them that you ask them any questions and they look over your shoulder. Flipo.

Chaotic and busy, good for history lovers. We had some ticket issues with the company we booked through but managed to get it all sorted before going up to have them scanned.

Unfortunately, I found it all too chaotic and busy, and also confusing to navigate where things actually were. Did get to see the Mona Lisa but it was far too busy to get close and appreciate it, as it was just like a herd of animals in one place with no organisation.

The staff were also quite rude and unhelpful, so I think some training wouldn’t go a miss particularly as you are dealing with people from all over the world. On the other hand staff in the cafes were polite and efficient.

Worth a visit but very crowded. I’m glad we visited the Louvre but it is quite an effort. Book tickets online days in advance as time slots sell out. We had to queue outside in heavy rain despite having time slot tickets. Many visitors were queuing outside for later time slots. On entering the museum it was unpleasantly busy and crowded everywhere. There was a second queue for security checks before entering the actual galleries. The main toilets in the entrance area also required queuing and were filthy - there are smaller toilets at various points in the galleries. Make sure to pick up a map before leaving the entrance as the building is huge and complex. Look out for the room numbers on doorways to find your bearings on the map. As expected, there was a very large crowd surrounding the Mona Lisa. The range of artwork and artifacts is impressive and make sure to visit the sculpture courtyard before leaving.

Very big - very busy. I knew this was the biggest museum in the world, but didn't know what this meant until I arrived. You need to arrive about an hour before your timed ticket if you don't want to lose half-an-hour queuing for your time slot. Once in through security, you don't get your ticket checked until you enter a wing of the museum, for which you have to queue again.

Through the ticket check, it depends which way you go - many people go straight to the Denon wing to see many of the famous pictures, including the Mona Lisa. This wing is massively crowded. Go this way if you want to, but it's worth going somewhere else - for example the foundations of the Louvre castle.

It is very much let down by lots of people guiding in the museum which leads to crowding in front of certain works.

As with other Paris museums, any food needs to be purchased in the museum at a premium. You can bring your own water in though. Not all the outlets seem to charge the same though.

There is too much to see in one day, but most of the crowds are in the Denon wing.

If you use the Louvre website there are a number of tours, but even though this is literally just a webpage, not an audioguide that needs each device updating, there were still items that were not on show but included in the tour.

In some places, don't forget to look at the room as well as the art.

Stolen antiquities.... It's definitely something you want to see closely when you come to Paris. However I want to say this, no people build history by showing some stolen antiquities that apparently belong to other cultures...

The staff are not always up to it.... Iconic and unmissable museum. The black spot is the public welcome. Courtesy of officers is required, which is quite normal. Unfortunately, some staff do not seem to apply it... lack of empathy, condescension, even aggression... It is a shame for the image of such a beautiful place.

Louvre once and never again. Coordination to the entrance went , but inside without structure or red thread . Except for the Mona Lisa, there are no good guides . Mass handling and many who only have to go to the Louvre once.

Alright but not worth the cost. Travelled hete during a bout of illness affecting mobility. There was works being undertaken at the time which were not advised making it harder to get around this extremely vast space. The toilets were also affectes during this time but signage was not updated at all, making it harder with kids. We did see some art but due to the effort and closedbattractions that we wanted to see (egyptian area for example) this detracted from the experience. We did manage to locate the mona lisa but there was such a vast aount of people that the only way the whole family could glimpse was via a photo taken by phone.
