We had audio guide but again this was limited about which pieces had a history to share. Overall maybe nicer if you had an entire day to kill, not mobility issue and was child free.

Too focused on the money. The museum clearly hugely over books the number of people it can comfortably accommodate through security. We had tickets for 0900 and eventually got into the museum at 0925 having queued for all that time. Muust be miserable if it's raining or really hot given there's cover. The museum is characterised by lots of people standing around staring at maps trying to figure out which way to go because it really isn't that clear. And there's no control at the major attractions so the Mona Lisa was just clogged with idiots taking insta selfies with no intervention from the staff. Really poor for such a major museum.

Mixed opinion. Mixed.

Date of visit: Monday, 23 February

Ticket for 11:30

An endless wait: fortunately there was the sun!

My little one wanted to go to the bathroom: a very nice lady outside made us pass in front of everyone. Which saved us a monumental amount of time. Thanks to her

Arrival at -2. Stink toilet! I find it borderline in a place like this.

Nothing indicated

Fortunately we ran into a very nice lady again who guided us.

The visit of the Richelieu alley went very well but it still lacks information / direction. And no one upstairs to ask for the news.

We ended up coming downstairs and asking for information from the same person who directed us well again.

But then a world at the joconde.... Impassable. People limit aggressive... we're gone.

So mixed on the visit because yes it is very beautiful, but it is sorely lacking in organization.
To see. Beautiful, to behold but shame is concomitant with the mall, loses a little charm.

Visit to the Louvre. Great exhibition

Very nice from the outside.

Despite tickets about 20 minutes waiting until you could finally get in.

However, a demonstration group was allowed in (and not thrown out). This meant that we had to wait about 30 minutes in front of the glass door (which was locked).

The museum itself (especially the Mona Lisa) are very worth seeing, but a little inscription was given.

Recommended length of stay: 5-6h

ok but too busy. Very busy even in early February. We joined the no ticket queue at 9am but got ina round 940am. Directions/signage was bad, signs would direct you to an exit but it wasnt a proper exit, just an exit out that room/section. Found this happened a lot. Difficult to find the main exit. Staff we encountered were not very helpful or welcoming. Once you are in the main museum there is only 1 cafe and its VERY expensive. You are better going back out to the main inner concourse and going down to main/lower level where you will find several cafes and shops. Beware there is not enough seating. Eat elsewhere out of the Louvre.
Not enough works explained with the audio guide. Overall there are many works but not enough explanations on the panels and the audio guide describes only 2 works per room.

A bit disappointed. We had pre-booked ticket for the minute they opened hoping to avoid the massive crowds. But when we arrived, due to the snow in Paris, after we got all the way to the inner courtyard (no idea why they did not alert people while walking in thru the entire front area) they announced the musuem was closed until 11am due to weather.

By the time we returned it was overly packed. There are very few bathrooms for such a massive museum and the wait for the ladies' room was extremely long.

Navigating around is so confusing - there is a map with numbers indicating areas and spaces but only twice did we find the number in the museum itself (and not just on the map).

The whole experience was crowded and pushy and frustrating (to find the areas you were looking for) and not the peaceful beautiful feel I would have wanted to have in a museum with such spectacular pieces of art to enjoy.

Exhausting. The Louvre is simply magnificent: extraordinary works, iconic settings and a collection that leaves you speechless. That said, the size is really huge and the visit can be exhausting, especially after a few hours. At some point it even becomes difficult to find your way around and finding the exit is not so immediate. An unmissable experience, but to be faced with time, comfortable shoes and maybe more separate visits.

A few nice pieces of art but bad overall experience. A few great pieces of art of course but service and experience were awful

Second floor closed, some stairs closed - which resulted in a highly inefficient visit, given we had to go back all the way we came. Poorly organised

No mention of the above at all neither when booking the 22-euro tickets nor on the day

Very few toilets and cues too long

Awful smell when we entered, likely coming from the toilets

Pretty. It’s so pretty at night. We went into the museum and had to queue for a while before finding out we did not have to queue due to unclear signs. We were rushed out of the Mona Lisa room it was very confusing. A lot of different unique arts inside.

Always full. Very full and is expected from the outside 1 hour, even with scheduled schedule. Inside is all very full. With lack of signage of the works or better and clearer indications of the pavilions. Giant, many stairs and paths.

Second time less inspiring.. This was my second visit to the Louvre and shared the experience with my wife.

We had a 1030 am entry time but due to unforeseen circumstances, we didn’t enter until just after 1200 pm.

We enjoyed our time but the museum is rather overwhelming in size. There are a few ‘gems’ that must be seen and experienced.

If you are short on time in Paris, skip the Louvre and go to the Musee d’Orsay instead!

Little available staff to provide information. Apart from the cashier, cloakroom, bar/restaurant workers and the La Mona Lisa painting area, many workers are constantly on the phone and very annoyed if you ask about the tours... I praise the few smiling, dedicated faces at work, but it would be enough to improve the signage/map/information screens and for those responsible to walk in those corridors. An unforgettable day among the wonders

Disarray but still a fabulous collection. The art is fabulous, full stop. This is a one-of-a-kind collection of world treasures. Our feedback is really about the Louvre staff and processes. We had timed tickets but still waited outside for more than an hour with a very disorganized process, small signage, and poor line control. Our entry time was long past our ticketed time. Frankly, it seemed the Louvre staff was disorganized and unprepared to handle such a crowd, though they do so daily. Inside the Louvre, galleries were (in predictable spots) completely overcrowded and unmanaged. The Mona Lisa queue was particularly disorganized -- instead of ropes/stanchions that would have given everyone an orderly, front-row view, it was a big v-shaped funnel that penned people in and did not provide more than a 25-person deep view for any who did not want to fight their way to the front. Of course, the wing that was robbed a few weeks ago was completely closed and sealed off, so our self-led audio guide got a little confusing until we figured that out. In some galleries, security guards seemed more interested in their cell phones than the crowds in the rooms. Case in point, the week we were there, a couple of Belgian Tik-Tok stars were able to hang a painting of themselves in a Lego frame in the same gallery as the Mona Lisa. It hung for more than a day before Louvre staff caught it (The museum says it was up only for minutes. Tik Tockers said otherwise).

For this treasure of a museum to be so disorganized was disappointing. I hope they figure it out sooner than later. Regardless, people will still queue for miles to see it. It's a treasure.

Only go if you really like museums. Would only recommend, if you really really like museums. We had reserved entrance, but still spent quite a while in line. The museum is huge and packed with people. We found it hard to navigate even when walking with the map. We spent a couple of hours, but then had to get out.

Great museum, but VERY hard to navigate. This is something you are required to do in Paris. Unfortunately, the only available tickets during our visit were 5:30pm on the last day we were there - so we were tired and our feet were worn out and it rained on us as we walked over and while we stood in line. So maybe not the perfect situation.

And there were SO many people there. Wow!

We got to see the famous exhibits - except the jewels that were stolen the week before our visit. I don't know if it was due to the theft, but there were many many places that were blocked so that you can't just walk in circles and see everything, but have to backtrack over and over. It is not easy to find your way around the museum and teh docents were very unhelpful - simply pointing to a sign that indicated we couldn't pass. Very disappointing. We finally gave up and just left.

Louvre was awesome to see BUT weird security interaction. I might get downvoted but I guess you had to be in my shoes to understand the feeling.

Louvre was wonderful and everything. I saw amazing things and I am pleased. Everyone who was helpful with questions and directions were so so kind but I had a weird and nasty interaction in the beginning. It was probably a language barrier but still chaotic and almost anxiety inducing. I am hoping no one paid attention because I was embarrassed.

Anyway, there was a small kiosk of sandwiches chips and different beverages. I was no where near the actual entrance of the line not even lining up. I was more toward the sidelines where people are sitting and eating but I had a plan to throw my stuff out before I line up. Some young security guy Monday, November 10 was trying to tell me to stop drinking it and hand it to him and I was like trying to tell him that I wasn’t going inside yet. I wasn’t lining up

Then he says "museum museum" but I point to the food area where I bought the drink to show him I will go there to finish it don’t worry. Before I go to the food area he tried to snatch (literally tried to wriggle it out of my hands) the canned drink from me which im not done with yet and im like oh no :( but i just paid for this, I was so shocked and so i pointed to the food area and started going toward there to finish the drink where everyone else is drinking and eating. As I was finishing it he ran to me and said “Why are you not listening?!” and I was like Why are you not listening? and then he snatched the empty can away from me like I was some child or thug. It was super weird. It made me feel sad but I tried not to let it get to me so l don't feel my day was ruined. Scary. I am a short small (lol) woman and totally thought his puny self was going to find a way to throw me out for finishing a drink I purchased from them a few feet away when all I wanted to do was finish it and throw it out in the nearby garbage. End rant. Thank you for reading and I love Paris by the way and can’t wait to be back. Just hope to to never ever see that security guy again. Absolutely awful.

Great exhibits just not a great experience. This was our least favourite place we visited in Paris. The price was very good and entry was very quick but it was over crowded and noisy inside. We started in the Sully wing which was quieter and very intersting but by the time we moved on to the Denon section our patience for the place was wearing thin. We finished off by seeing the Mona Lisa which was a bit of an anti climax to the visit. There are much better pieces of art in the Museum. Trying to get out of the Louvre was a nightmare to be honest.

Get in early and avoid peak season. Bought a ticket for 9 AM entry and got in without any issues. The Mona Lisa was a bit crowded but I didn’t go there to see the painting. The art collection was very impressive and the French are very good at dressing things up. I definitely would not visit during the peak season. 3 stars because of the small number of bathrooms.

It was good. The museum is very busy and worth a visit. Amazing art work, lots to see. It was very difficult to get close to the monalisa as there were crowds.

The trip advisor voucher was so difficult to download as it wasn’t showing and I had to contact support to help me. The code that shows on your booking is not your ticket!

The Louvre’s art deserves better. An operational mess that ruins the magic of Paris. If you want to set yourself up for the least enjoyable experience of Paris, head to the Louvre!

I went in fully aware there would be crowds, but I was still shocked by how poorly managed the experience was. Filthy toilets, queues at every turn, staff who seemed apathetic and burnt out at best, and openly hostile at worst. This was on a random Monday in October, not even peak season.

Cut capacity in half and double the ticket price. At least then it might feel somewhat civilized. It’s honestly no wonder this place was once robbed in broad daylight given the state of operations.

Three stars purely for the art and masterpieces it houses. Otherwise, it’s an operational nightmare.

People say, “Well, it’s the Louvre, you have to do it.” But actually, no, you don’t.
Medium. There is a very bad organization because we took more than 1h30 to get home despite buying tickets online and it was very hard to do the tour properly with everyone

Unparalleled wealth mismanaged. The vastness and importance of the collections, with breathtaking works of art, are not supported by proper organization. There is no question of visiting the Louvre, because it is essential for anyone in Paris: the extraordinary quantity and variety of masterpieces it holds does not allow replicas. What is disputed is a poor management, it is true that the museum is immense and therefore more complicated, but improving the usability is necessary. The visit of the collections, spread over five levels and in three interconnecting wings, is complex even for those who have planned it in advance. Moreover, even if you enter by time slots (in this regard, the ticket must be purchased online at least a week in advance, otherwise you risk not finding availability), the most popular rooms are overcrowded. Why put the most famous painting in the world together with other extraordinary works? It goes for “The Wedding of Cana” by Paolo Veronese, which being very large you can appreciate, but for other works we had to wait for the line for the Mona Lisa to be cleared. What is more, we found the Egyptian Antiquities Hall closed; although restorations are necessary, a small exhibition could have been organized in other rooms, since in one sector of the upper floors there are completely empty rooms. In summary, the visit is a must, but the organization not up to scratch compromises the excitement of being in front of unique works in the world.

Destination that sells fast. Visited a few days ago

We didn’t know about the extra included in the ticket

Unfortunately we arrived late and did not have the photo with Gighen

I report drafts of air from some windows not closed properly

Too bad for the inconveniences.. The two stars are awarded only for the wonders found there.

For the rest, I experienced disorganization: from the few and not very clean restrooms (thus, always accompanied by inevitable lines), to the few seating areas, to the difficulty of understanding the purpose of the visit and the layout of the various rooms. In short, a tour of the Museum can prove complicated also due to the enormous crowding and the heat that reigns in some rooms (especially that of the Mona Lisa and David).

be patient with your troubles!. There is an abyssal gap between the quality of the works and the catastrophic management of the entries. Year after year, as a Parisian, I have seen how much access to the museum has deteriorated. It is a total mess to access it, with completely inadequate control installations. The Louvre is dying from its success!

Don’t plan a visit for a Monday morning. Had 9:30 booked tickets. Learned that the workers strike Monday mornings and determine when they want to open. No communication with the crowd who were told to stand in line with no shade or protection from the sun. We didn’t get inside until approximately 11:30. We didn’t know if we should stay or leave as we had no idea when it would open. Bathrooms were dirty, no soap on many dispensers. The art was beautiful but the workers have no pride in their national treasure and treat their visitors poorly. Many sections of the museum are under construction and closed.

Disappointing. I was really excited to go but it was quite a let down. It felt like a bunch of art was just crammed into a space. No curation, no logic, no journey. In contrast to the Prado in Madrid, I think the Louvre really needs to pull up their socks

Above all, do not go there if your child is in a stroller!. Of course, the place is culturally fascinating, but the accessibility for people with reduced mobility and babies in strollers is a shame.

40 minutes to exit the museum! Absurd signage; the goal seems to be to prevent visitors from using the elevators (which are insufficient in number).

Was interesting, but.... Well, it is underwhelming at best. Management is poor, exhibitions are not properly presented. Signs are not clear, one can easily get lost. Employees are not always polite. And yes, this has to be said,..toilets...they are filthy, dirty, smell disgusting and are in very poor condition, i.e. water does not work, soap dispenser does not work, hand dryer does not work etc. People entering have to cover their nose to be able to stay and some just leave. Why one of the most famous museums in the world cant invest in presentable clean toilets is beyond me. This is a huge issue in many museums in France, in general. And it just spoils overall impression. Also, some galleries/rooms are simply closed without warning. So, one does not know what they can actually see when buying tickets. Separate mention about crowds to see Mona Lisa. Really, this could be managed better.

It's better to book (in advance) the Musée d'Orsay.. I must say that I tried to purchase tickets from my phone on the official website, and it was impossible due to the ridiculous procedure to follow (so it is better to buy them from a PC and/or from home). I resorted - like many others - to the "Tickets Louvre" website, which is the first one that appears when typing "Louvre Tickets," and they sent me fake tickets, already used by someone else. I therefore had to repurchase the tickets inside, where there is a ticket office for cases like mine evidently...

Since it seems that this issue is affecting many, the staff was already well prepared for it...

I wonder: why, knowing about these repeated frauds (there is also a warning on the official website), doesn't the Louvre set up to sell tickets directly?

It smells like a "scam" if I have to be honest.....

As for the museum, there are about 30/40 beautiful works, but many are of secondary importance. The Mona Lisa is obviously a "superstar," impossible to get close without a controlled flow path.

Four hours, the memory of which (if no photos are taken) is just of a marathon in a crowd like a fruit and vegetable market, given the thousands of people inside.

Book the Musée d'Orsay in advance....better.

You are visiting Paris; given the crowd, I am not sure it is worth it.... Misery!!! The crowd, we are not far from the Vatican Museum. Even with reserved tickets, we had to wait in line for half an hour before passing through security, where we were yelled at by the security agents. Tip: two entrances, one at the forecourt, the other through the carousel; in case of rain, at least it is sheltered.

Given the crowd and its tourists, bravo to the luggage service; remove this service from the website, to find it is not intuitive, and for 20 unfortunate lockers (like at the pool) that are transparent (good, if we have a valuable bag, it is designed to tempt thieves because it is easy to force the lockers, one was actually out of order), let's just say there is no space! We had to find a luggage service outside! Go to the Royal Gallery in London, where, moreover, the entrance is free; there is a physical luggage service on site...

In short, once inside, finally, take a map beforehand because it is not easy to navigate! Moreover, to find the elevators or escalators, it is a scavenger hunt.

Many rooms are closed, and not just the Crown Jewels room. In conclusion, the Raft of the Medusa, Liberty Leading the People, could not be seen!

Fortunately, we did not pay the high price for tourists outside the EU! Be careful when booking online not to pay the high price! By the way, they only check the free entries (my son under 25 had to show an ID, we did not).

Otherwise, look up at the beautifully adorned ceilings! On the other hand, stars like the Venus de Milo, the Mona Lisa, and the Winged Victory of Samothrace are overshadowed by this crowd of common people. Furthermore, to manage this crowd, they have established one-way traffic, so you cannot even go back.

The restrooms, before entering, require payment, and inside there is yet again an overwhelming crowd, with half of the facilities out of order and staff yelling at you!

In short, a nightmare; one wonders how, with such a flow of people, they are not able to maintain it properly.

A sumptuous museum with undignified reception conditions.. How did the greatest museum in the world get here? Reception conditions more than deplorable. How can one maintain a single security gantry when one receives a visit from autan de monde! Moreover, it is high time to remove the interfaces from the Stone Age. Just like the scenography!

Very big disappointment. It is unacceptable to close rooms, as important as those in the Egyptian collections, without warning

We came from Nantes on purpose with my 2 grandchildren and extremely disappointed

Especially since the guards told us that this often happens

In a company, we know how to organize to satisfy customers, so why not at the Louvre museum?
Very long wait. To visit the museum can be very hard for anyone infirme or elderly. With timed tickets and arriving on time, we had an hour'’s wait to be admitted.

Skip It. I have been to the Louvre multiple times over the last 20 years and honestly each time I go it gets worse and worse. Currently the workers are striking on and off and the way the museum is dealing with it is ridiculous and antiquated. I was emailed a few days before our visit that the museum may open late or be closed. I monitored the website and their social media the night before and in the morning before opening and received no further indication of the status of the museum. Since we had 0900 tickets we had to be there at (well actually before) opening in case they did open. It isn't like we could sit in our hotel and wait until later to see if they opened. We got all the way to the Louvre to find a sign saying they would be closed for now... check back later. You couldn't put that in a mass email? On the website? Social media? Instead the museum staff there said- come back later, we might be open. What?

We decided to head to Notre Dame in the meantime and I kept checking the website when finally they did open. We made it back around 12:30 and I asked (after being told by staff in the AM that we would be able to skip the lines since we had 09 entry) where to go. We were sent on a huge round about, walk here- nope they won't let you in, go here, nope go there. I had my son and older parents with me and they were exhausted from being sent all over the place. How can things be so disorganized? Finally I spoke with someone else and she said I had to just wait in the line with the people who have the 12:30/1:00 timed tickets. But I was told I would get priority? Nope. She didn't care and breezed right past me when I asked. So we waited, eventually made it into the museum, and it was packed. All the people who had AM tickets were there with those with early afternoon tickets. I did send a complaint to the museum about all of this and while they responded quickly it was basically a- well here the law says we can do it this way so we do answer. Perhaps, but as the largest and most visited museum in the world you can do better.

While this museum contains amazing artifacts, paintings, statues, furniture, etc it is just too big. It isn't fun to see these things because people are pushing and shoving to see them. Honestly I would support banning phones and picture taking all together. No one is appreciating the art for what it is- they want their selfie and to move onto the next thing.

Interestingly enough they just raised the prices for people who live outside the EU... it isn't worth the entry price. Read about the pieces you want to see online. Unfortunately the Louvre has become a circus and you are paying too much for the hassle you get. Go to the Orsay instead.

Disorganization at the top. Works and structure are not discussed

But the organization is embarrassing

Inadequately managed and dirty toilets

Too much unmanaged turnout with moments of chaos

The location of the rooms is confusing and references are often missing

The apotheosis is before the Mona Lisa

A mass of people with mobile phones without a path logic to drain

Management should take courses in Italian museums to learn

RUDE STAFF. this grown lady in one of the louvre shops was mad cause I was sitting down looking at something in the store on the floor and grabbed me and pushed me to a corner. I was about to cry I’m literally a child what the hell!?! and my sisters knee was over the barrier and she didn’t realize since she has a bone disorder and a man yelled at her and and patted her shoulder super hard and yelled at her like hello? Idk why maybe cause we speak English here but we’re literally Hispanic either way it doesn’t matter the hell!?!

Great Museum, Useless & Mean Outside Staff. The staff at the Louvre were rude and obnoxious. I tried to buy my ticket online in advance as suggested. But for some reason, the site was only allowing me to purchase a 65€ ticket with audioguide, which I did not want. So I went to the museum and thought I could purchase the 32€ ticket on site. When I arrived, it was fortunately not very crowded and there were a bunch of museum workers outside.

I asked about buying my ticket and a disenchanted worker claimed we had to scan a QR code.

Upon doing so, you are sent on a wild goose chase to get your ticket!! The QR code leads you to an app that demands that you create an account with your address, phone number with country code, email address, date of birth in European format, etc... That day, their app was not working and kept sending me back to input my info all over again. I attempted over 5 times and wasted an hour! When I told one nasty French museum worker the app was not working, she just shrugged and claimed it was not working for a lot of people with no attempt to resolve the situation. Another even nastier and moronic French museum worker said that the app didn't like password with numbers even though the instructions insist you include them. He also lied to me that I would just have to come back another day as that was the only way to get a ticket!

This was all just the nastiness of the Parisian French. Because they have ticket booths inside! Finally after an hour and 15 minutes, one of the workers announced that we could line up to go in and purchase tickets at the ticket booths inside the museum.

This was sheer stupidity and nastiness. Many people left because the app was not working and the museums workers were LYING to people and telling us there was no other way to get a ticket. As soon as anyone said they could not get the QR code/app to work, the museum workers should have immediately sent us downstairs to buy our tickets. What was the sense in making people try over and over and then LYING to us that the QR code was the only way to get a ticket?? I heard some museum employees were on strike. Were these workers trying to sabotage the museum by costing it money??? They are robbing it just like the robbers who stole art work. Perhaps the Louvre HR needs to do better background checks and training of employees!
Long queue, closed at short notice, no admission possible. Unfortunately was closed at short notice (5min before), long wait lines unnecessarily we were all denied entry...and nobody cared!!

Take pictures outside and pass inside. It was the historic but to me it is over rated. Not at all what I thought. Just a big tourist trap!I would just get pictures outside. I saw lots of things. But it is extremely overrated ! it is extremely crowded for not much I cared about. However that box was checked. I would pass on this one.

Just skip it. It was the historic but to me it is over rated. I would just get pictures outside. I saw lots of things. But it is extremely overrated the French are extremely rude there and it is extremely crowded for not much I cared about. However that box was checked. I would pass on this one
Bad Experience. Amazing museum with so much to see and worth a visit,but terrible customer service.Very long line.No good information.

At the Louvre, thieves come home easier than visitors!. A scandal! We had made a reservation Thursday, February 20 for 10 am, and 1 hour wait after, we learn, by questioning an agent that there is a strike and that the decision makers were in meeting to decide whether or not to open the museum. There were hundreds of us, in the rain and cold, with children and foreign tourists who had come thousands of kilometers to see this "institution" that until now was the Louvre. After 2 hours of waiting, always in the same conditions, we cracked and went elsewhere! We tried, wherever we were in Paris to contact them during the day, not even an information message, nothing. It is not the strike that bothers me, I think that if the employees of the Louvre do it, they have good reasons and I think they do. It is the TOTAL lack of information by the leaders, on YES or NO, they opened to the hundreds of people outside and who had all paid their places, to avoid waiting for nothing. Some angry tourists shouted messages to the people at the museum who seemed to be going out for a break, and some even smiled at the situation! No wonder the wolf is in this state, it is not at all up to such a museum. France and the FR must really be seen as truffles in the image of the world, it is a total disgrace. The leaders of the Louvre need to get their act together and question themselves, just because you will always have tourists does not mean that you should disrespect them to this extent. Now I'm waiting for a refund and I'm not gonna let them go...

Accessibility for people with the. Hundreds of elevators, many rooms closed, but no signposts, you find out after half an hour of walking that you can not proceed and you have to go back. For a wheelchair user it's a nightmare! In 4 hours we were able to see only the Italian painting room and part of the Egyptian the rest of the time spent in countless elevators that make you climb 1 meter... I strongly advise against people with reduced mobility.

Watch out for ticketing. For citizens from Switzerland, the entrance fee is Eur 32 p/p, for members of the EEA (European Economic Area, Switzerland is not a member) Euro 22.-- If you do not study the conditions for purchasing a ticket in advance precisely enough, chances are high that you will receive an EEA ticket because, as in our case, the ticket counter does not ask for the identity of a person. Although a flyer with prices is attached to the side of the counter, it does not automatically catch your eye and the staff does not ask for it accordingly. Consequence: On arrival, regular checks are carried out by marking purchased tickets as not valid. The staff will then request identification. If it turns out that you have an EEA ticket as a CH citizen, admission is refused and you are directed back to the counter. There you pay the difference (Euro 10 p/p) plus a penalty of Euro 5.-p/p for buying the wrong ticket. In our case, my wife was not stopped with the "wrong" ticket, but I was. When asked why my wife came through, unlike me, we received no reply and were rather unkindly referred back to the ticket counter.
Mediocre experience. Too dispersive. Useless audio guide: offers no advice on possible routes and provides little information about the works. A museum cannot live by nomea alone.

It would be an amazing experience if they made it easy. It's the best and worst museum in the world.

Difficult, complicated, museum supervisors are unfriendly and 0 participatory and eager to help!

They close and open parts of the museum without warning.

Complicated to handle inside with 0 indications but at the same time it is something amazing

Shame for the disorganization. Non-existent organisation at the entrance system. You book a slot online but is completely useless: prepare to do at least two hours of queue. By the time you will access the museum, you'll be tired already. Book the earliest slot possible because, if you book the afternoon, you will have to leave by the time you manage to enter.

Scam?. I love art, I love museums, I love the Louvre's content. The place is poorly run. It is far too crowed. The museum should admit fewer number of people at one time so people can actually enjoy the experience. What a sham. What a scam. I had been before, in years past, when you could actually enjoy yourself. No one enjoys being squished into a sardine can. And many areas were closed off too. And they didn't open due to strikes but kept our money. What a scam.

Disgusted. We came here on purpose to see the second floor dedicated to painters but despite a normal opening day, the second floor was closed due to lack of staff. Except that nothing is indicated on the website when booking tickets. And there's nothing on site, just straps blocking access.

And what about the personnel for information, not cordial. It seems the staff is jaded.

Too bad for this exceptional place.

I'm not going back. Yeah, well, six hours of driving for that is annoying.

A place without security where you can hurt yourself without anyone intervening.. Disabled (I walk with a prosthesis) and aged 71 I fell while sliding on a wet floor. I hurt myself very badly and my husband searched in vain in more than six rooms: not a single security person. No elevator nearby. No arrow no indication no room number to orientate.... It was foreign tourists who helped us. Where are the guards who used to be there for the second?

Over crowding spoilt the experience. We have visited the Louvre before and were looking forward to seeing the magnificent and varied collections. We had booked an entrance ticket for 1:30 on a Wednesday. On arrival by the Pyramid entrance we joined a long queue. After around twenty minutes we passed through security and descended into the main atrium which was thronged with people. Trying to reach any of the exhibits proved challenging as the building was so over-crowded. After around forty minutes we decided to leave as it was impossible to fully appreciate the exhibits due to the sheer number of people.

More than an hour of delay in opening the Louvre. It is 29 sept 2025, it is 10.10 am, Louvre was supposed to be open ay 9.00 but it has not opened yet. Thousands of people waiting. Hard to feel happy when looking at Gioconda.

Dreamy artistic heritage but bad internal organization. This is my third time at the Louvre but I have to say that the experience from the 90s to today has gradually worsened. The artistic heritage of this museum is very rich but the organization definitely needs to be improved.

To begin the Welcoming inside the pyramid we were given conflicting directions on the staircase from which to climb (we were directed under one of the stairs and arrived there, we were told we had made a mistake and they made us go back).

The plants provided at the entrance and the signs inside the museum are not at all clear and it was impossible to follow a linear path but several times we went up and down in search of the sectors that interested us wasting time and energy.

Finally, access is allowed to really too many visitors at once, making it impossible to fully enjoy what is on display due to the crowds and the rumor.

Getting close to the Mona Lisa was impossible. My husband and I had been able to observe it in much better conditions and we gave up but my children really wanted to see it so they stood in a pseudo line and when they managed to get close the staff there immediately let them out without having time to take a look.

Our family really likes to go for museums and we have visited them all over the world with vast artistic heritage.... I can say without a shadow of a doubt that the Louvre is the one with the worst organization and this makes the experience much less pleasant than you would expect.

My next time in Paris, with great regret, I will not be going back.

Extremely disappointing. 3 days stay in Paris, where everything was perfect except the visit of the Louvre to our great regret. Excessive heat in most of the museum’s rooms (except the foundation part of the medieval Louvre). A very bad crowd management in front of the best known works, notably Mona Lisa, where shoving, malaise took place and even the beginning of a fight! Finally, our tickets were scheduled for entry at 4:30 p.m., and several accesses closed under our noses by the staff without any explanation. We could not access for example the Egyptian antiques (it was 5:30 pm), or the view from inside at the pyramid, again we were blocked access at the bottom of the stairs, without explanation, it was only 6:15 pm. That's a shame.

Requires physical ID for headsets. Poor customer service and organisation let down a wonderful collection

The Louvre’s art collection is undeniably breathtaking—one of the finest in the world. The guided audio tour purchased directly through the museum was itself informative and well-structured, offering fascinating insight into the museum’s rich history, architecture, and many of its most iconic masterpieces. On the strength of the collection and the content of the tour alone, this review would be 5 stars.

However, serious issues with staff attitude and poorly communicated museum policies significantly impacted our experience and compel me to rate this just 2 stars.

Most importantly, be aware: the museum requires a physical form of government-issued ID (e.g., passport or driver’s licence) to be left with them in exchange for the audio tour equipment. This was not made clear at the time of booking, and the tickets simply stated “ID required.” We had digital ID (which is now a legitimate and accepted form of identification in countries like Australia), but this was flatly rejected. When we politely asked to speak to a manager, the staff member was rude, dismissive, and outright refused our request.

Only after some persistence with other members of staff were we able to speak to a departmental manager, who finally agreed to make an exception—on the basis that I wear two hearing aids and without the audio headset, the tour would have been inaccessible to me. This situation caused considerable stress and was entirely avoidable had the museum clearly communicated their policy.

Additionally, the Mona Lisa room was chaotic—no queueing system, severe overcrowding, and no crowd control. It took over an hour just to reach a spot where we could see the painting. When we raised concerns about the disorganisation, the staff present were again dismissive and rude, showing no interest in improving the visitor experience.

Finally, the gallery signage and maps were poor and made it unnecessarily difficult to find specific works or to navigate by art movement or period.

With the vast resources and international standing of the Louvre, better customer service, clearer communication, and more thoughtful organisation should be the norm. Sadly, these shortcomings marred what should have been a highlight of our trip.

To flee on Monday. Negative review due to overcrowding.

I couldn’t wait to visit this famous museum, take the time to stop in front of a canvas.

Except that July and especially Monday are absolutely to be avoided. It’s crowded and I couldn’t wait to get out of this hell.

French tourists were not the most disrespectful.

I had booked a guided tour. The guide was great and that I recommend. A guided tour changes everything.

The downside of the guided tour is the fighter’s journey to find the rendezvous point. Even if the museum staff is really welcoming and kind to guide us it is not nice to galley so much.

I will return in the low season.

Poor website and unprganized museum. Beautiful museum and beautiful paintings.

Horribly difficult website, specially, for a tourist to book anything, unhelpful staff, extremely unorganized museum with a useless map for a visitor, dirty and smelly bathrooms, etc.

Extremely easy to get lost as there are ways to everywhere! How hard is it to print a simple walk though map for a visitor from the most busiest museum in Paris?

Learn from the Italians!. Aware of entering one of the greatest temples of art, I expected an organization equal to so much superlative beauty. Surveillance, in fact, is really poor: operators, where present, allowed the noise of patrons Works without safety devices, overcrowding and market scenes. Far from Italian standards

Incredibly unprofessional management and untenable conditions. An imposition!. If you love squeezing through the exhibitions with thousands and thousands of people, this is the place for you. The chaos begins as soon as you enter the house, even with previously purchased timeslot tickets you have to wait in a 200m long line for at least 1h. Then a tour of poorly air-conditioned or non-air-conditioned premises with constant crowding and scrambles. Poor signage and labels of the exhibits only in French. A visit to a museum, you would not wish for it! For us, this visit was not the first, but certainly the last.

Welcoming not up to it. A magnificent museum with incredible works... but we are in Paris, unkind, even unpleasant staff, who do not give a damn about visitors, etc. Welcoming no match for such a place... we expect more tolerance, understanding, etc.

Brace yourself!. €88 for four of us, so not cheap though it is the world's largest museum, in a palace, so...

We booked timed tickets, which worked well. The few staff we encountered (three out whole visit 😤) were lovely and helpful, but there weren't nearly enough staff to ask questions or directions etc.

The whole place is amazing, but seems far busier than it should be and some things haven't improved during my lifetime of visits...you won't get near the Mona Lisa, so honestly don't bother trying, you'll need binoculars to even see it!

The map and signage are awful and you'll spend much of your time just trying to find what you're looking for.

It took us nearly fifteen minutes walking to find out way out!

There's nowhere near enough seats, toilets or places to get refreshments...they really need ten times as many!

Despite charging millions of international visitors who can read English, you'll rarely find anything written in English and many exhibits don't have ANY explanation in any language, which we all found really frustrating.

After several hours (mostly lost) we left with aching feet, all vowing to never return!

If you love museums, spend a day or two marvelling at the building and exhibits...but if you're hoping to get around in two hours don't bother...the crappy map and signage alone add that to your visit.

Enjoy

Disappointing. If you want to feel like cattle in cattle car this is place. Hot, crowded, long lines for toilets which are hard to find. It’s now wonder staff have gone on strike. The management of the queue for the Mona Lisa is ridiculous. The experience completely took away from the amazing pieces of art and history that is there. Unless this genre of art and specific pieces are something you’re desperate to see I’d say skip it for one of the many other museums.

Strong perfume scent 😰 Poor ventilation facility. This is my second visit to the Louvre. It used to be a five-star facility. It was different from the last time.

Popular art places are crowded and crowded and smell very perfumed. I thought perfume was useless in the museum. Is ventilation also bad? I can't smell a place with so few people. Coconut scents are popular in France among dark-skinned women. Even a female employee in a black uniform wore perfume. The high-grade scent of men was the one I was allergic to. The female coconut incense was fine.

Perfume vaporizes and chemicals affect the mucous membranes of the skin, eyes, nose, and mouth. My nose and head and eyes hurt. My mouth is numb. Vaporized chemicals travel from the lungs through blood vessels to the brain. Viewers are encouraged to refrain from wearing vaporized perfume to feel safe as it adheres to their bodies, clothing and artworks. If it smells too thick, it causes allergies and makes it sick and difficult to breathe. You can only pass by quickly in a short time, and the most popular places where you took a break and came in less time are only for a moment or a short time so as not to inhale too much perfume quickly.

I hope that the companies that make perfumes will also develop and market perfumes for museums in crowded spaces that do not cause allergies.

The smell of perfume, this was different.

The Mona Lisa has a sad history of being attacked.

People in wheelchairs have low viewing heights, so it is difficult to see the paintings because the light reflects off the pictures, but the Mona Lisa is displayed low and was devised so that people with disabilities and wheelchairs can easily view it.

Unlike in the past, the able-bodied were the Mona Lisa, who could only be seen from afar. Many staff members were very kind to me in my wheelchair.

Nike has a staircase, but if you want to see it from the side, you can easily go and see it with a wheelchair. There was a small staircase which was not easily accessible by wheelchair at the front or opposite side.

The Venus of Milo is quite difficult to get to in a wheelchair and there are few signage. There were many gates that prohibited going backwards. Two staff members were very kind to us. There were a lot of stairs and the elevators were shaded, so it was complicated, going up and down, going around the same place looking for the lack of stairs, and when we opened first in line in the morning, we went right in, but at last we ran out of time and had an appointment for the next tour of the opera and couldn’t see it. The smell of thick perfume caused headaches and nausea, so I wanted to appreciate it and get out quickly. I scrambled to put on my mask after I got sick, but it was too late. We took a break for a while in a well-ventilated area with a map or a small window to catch our breath.

I finally couldn't get to see Venus of Milo. I felt sick and I was going to throw up, but I couldn't get there after all the hard work.

I want people to be able to see Milo's Venus in a straight, straight, barrier-free way without stairs. I wanted to make it easier for the staff to speak to me if I wanted to use many information signs and if I was prohibited from going backwards or entering. I thought it was a difficult facility for people in wheelchairs to go all the way from Japan and not be able to see it when they had plenty of time.

The black-clad staff doesn't appear to be museum curators or specialists. They may be secur

Musee du Louvre. We were sad and disappointed to find how exhausting our visit to this world-famous art museum turned out to be. Nowhere in our pre-visit reading were we alerted to the physically demanding amounts of stair steps you must climb up and down in order to see your most-desired artistic displays. While there are a few escalators to be found in the more modern entrance, the majority of exhibits we wanted to see were only accessible by climbing up flight after flight of long and steep staircases. On one or more occasions after having climbed a number of these staircases, I finally had to make the decision not to attempt any further climbing since we had no idea about how many additional sets of these staircases it was going to take. We finally had to give up any further Louvre exploration in order to have enough energy to visit the many other wonderful sights in Paris we had wanted to see.

Public toilet nightmare. Without several hours to spare, we skipped the museum and wandered the grounds. Some nice installations available for free.

Under the Louvre there is a shopping mall with some high end and some souvenir shops. We required the toilet and had to pay a Euro to visit the smelliest, dirtiest, most crowded, least private and busiest public room in Paris. Avoid this stop unless it's an emergency! This darling old dear had to collect the Euros from increasingly grumpy tourists all day long. Poor man!
Poorly managed influx. The flow of people is too high, you can not see the works for too many people. I would stay there again at a different time.

Underwhelming Unfortunately. This is something I looked forward to for my visit to Paris. Unfortunately, my experience was underwhelming at best. The best part of my visit to the Louvre was observing the architecture of the place, reason being-the museum was INCREDIBLY crowded. People pushed and shoved their way through the Denon gallery to the point that it was difficult to enjoy the art. Staff and maps were also not specific or helpful. Seeing the Mona Lisa was difficult to do. There was no organization or order to the mass of people wanting to see her, so we had to jockey our way through a massive crowd. We quickly took a picture. After spending thirty seconds at the front of the massive crowd, we were asked to move on. FINALLY, we had a 2:30 ticket. We got in line at 2:30, but we didn’t enter the museum until 3:30 because the line was so long and security was so slow..

#dontwasteyourtime. If you enjoy standing in line while time melts away and your coffee buzz fades, this is the place for you! Had 9am tickets—still outside at 10am. I brought someone who’s never been to this city… and now they think chaos is the main attraction. Save yourself. See literally any other museum. Or just wander aimlessly, it’ll be more productive.

Mixed opinion. Very nice building. What a waste of time due to lack of signage. Disappointment to access the Mona Lisa...difficult with children because adults are too busy taking selfies and do not see the little ones.

Big problems in accessing toilets for women

Busy and smelly. Disappointing experience. SO SO SO busy, over an hour queueing to get in despite having a booking time, neverending queues for the bathroom, 30mins queueing for a bottle of water from the self service restaurant. There was a terrible smell of toilets/ sewers in some parts of the building too, even when there were no bathrooms near by. Very disappointing for this so called famous and prestigious museum.

Beautiful but not dancing. Obviously incredible museum, wonderful works of art and many.

Unfortunately, the grade is lowered for some specific things:

\- lack of an organized and clear path (a real shame because you go from one place to another without logic)

\- impractical and very confusing audio guide

\- some works are inaccessible (the crowd of people around the Mona Lisa is really embarrassing, it would take little to organize flows and make sure that you can see clearly)

\- for the size and quantity of works is very dispersive and often some incredible works (eg Love and Psyche of Canova) are really undervalued, a shame

\- embarrassing the change of the names of Italian painters in their French “correspondent”. I found it frankly ridiculous

\- very poor sanitation for the amount of people present

Positive notes:

\- friendly staff

\- magnificent works of art

\- good management of the flow of incoming people despite there were many was respected our entry time. Not obvious I must say.

A Great Way to Experience Tourists.. Welcome to a tourist trap.

I have been going to the Louvre for 45 years. It was always a place to visit when in Paris. I still remember the first time I saw the Mona Lisa. It was a painting hanging on the side wall between two other da Vinci works. That was reality.

Now, you are in a free-for-all with all the tourists in town. Time Square is empty on New Years Eve. The process to get in on a selected time was fine,. You had to do this. But then the lines inside got worse. That process took 1 hours. Finally, after talking to one of the desk associates I decided it was time to leave. This was not a museum. It was an amusement park.

Of course, you have to walk through what looks like an outlet mall with lots of shops and places in the passage way. That was just as bad. And over priced.

I was glad to leave. The next time in Paris I will pick another museum if I want. This time I went to the CLUNY. I have been going for 45 years as well. That was empty and you can see the works of art there at your own pace.

Merci to the Louvre. It was an experience.

Pure chaos for €33. An absolute outrage. You pay €33 or €23 if you are outside of EU to enter the most disorganised, badly marked, stuffy and filthy museum on earth. 9 million people visits this place every year. That’s around €20m revenue just from the tickets, not counting donations, government grants, Friends of Louvre, gift shop and cafes revenues and profits from art images usage. For that enormous amount of money they can’t bother to put proper signs to direct the audience to places other then the highly overrated Mona Lisa, English descriptions of the art from majority of sections (ancient Egypt, French painters, Ancient Greece etc) or even clean toilets more often than every two hours, when you have an ever age of almost 30000 visitors per day! I’m attaching a photo of one of the ‘hidden’ toilets that I used on the day. Waiting time of 20min and an absolute filth. Staff couldn’t get more bored and disinterested and don’t get me started on the fact they should be clearing out rooms 30min before closing, but in reality they will kick you out from less visited sections when they want - because they want to wrap up quicker and go home. Louvre is no longer a must see place in Paris - it’s a survival camp.
NOISY AS HELL?????. DISGUSTING!!?!!!??.!!!!???????!!???Air conditioner DIDN'T work when it was over 90 degrees outside?????

Still waiting for refund after 4 months. My husband and I were in France for our honeymoon in February, but our Louvre visit was cancelled due to a public strike. We were not allowed to reschedule and were told to submit a refund request instead.

I was later informed that my refund had been approved, but it is now June. 4 months later, I still have not received it as it has not been processed yet. As an international traveller, I cannot be physically present in France to follow up, and my repeated emails asking about the refund have been ignored.

This has left a very negative impression of the Louvre and how they handled the whole situation. The delay, lack of communication, and poor refund process were extremely disappointing. Based on this experience, I would advise other travellers to be cautious and avoid visiting if there is any risk of cancellation or disruption, as getting a refund can be extremely difficult.

Paris's biggest disappointment. Planned and pre-ticketed as a must visit for first trip to Paris. Sadly, the Louvre proved to be the only disappointment in Paris. The stunning array of artworks in the magnificent building were impossible to enjoy in the midst of dense crowds, and the noise was oppressive in many areas. The Louvre appeared over developed and commercially driven to accommodate crowds in high volume with little regard for the quality of experience. Poorly signposted and mapped - if this were improved, the wandering lost souls might reduce bottlenecks. Musee D'Orsay a much better experience.
I won’t be using Trip Advisor again.. The correct tickets were not sent out to me, causing undue delays and stress.

Don’t go on Monday morning. Do not go on a Monday morning. There are very often delayed openings for one reason or another. We got there at 9.45 for our 10.00 booked entry to find 9.00 queue still there and not moving. We were told that there was a meeting going on and they would probably open about 10.30. That would potentially mean we would not be in until 11.30. It was hot the sun was beating down and we had afternoon plans so we didn’t stay to go in. We are not entitled to a refund from the Louvre or out tour group.
Outrageous behavior. Simply opened over 1.5 hours late, with no information to the ticket holders.

Lack of respect. During record heatwave weather, we wanted to escape the heat by visiting the Louvre. Except that we have to wait under the scorching sun in line to buy our tickets... with the heat that Paris experiences every summer, I find it really disrespectful not to provide at least some shelter so that visitors do not wait in the sun. In short, we let it happen...

Beware re ticket puchases. I spent 30 euro by taxi to visit this site arriving before the 9 am opening time. I checked the official website which indicted to be cautious of ticket scammers and recommended purchasing at the museum. However, upon arrival, there was a queue for “ticket holders.” I approached a male representative who advised me to wait over by a small group of people who had gathered for entry at 10:30?! However, i should know “that the ticket office may not open today.”

It was already a hot day and to expect people to wait in line to maximize ticket sales is incredulous. Moreover, no such practice was mentioned on the website!

The experience did not contribute to my earlier negative experiences the day before. Such inconsideration and i will not again visit this city.

Travellers should purchase tickets online so as to avoid paying hefty taxi fees and not be guaranteed entry.

Good luck!

horrible customer service. horrible customer service. charged me for 2 tickets because of their mistake and Francois from online ticket sales refused to issue a refund and couldn't care less. sad that they know people will still visit no matter how horrible they treat paying customers.

RESTROOMS QUITE DIRTY FOR A MUSEUM OF THIS CALIBER.. How can it be that one of the most important museums in the world has such a low level of cleanliness? Unbearable smell, unhygienic conditions, and long lines in the women's restrooms.

A significant lack of cleanliness, poor management, and very low cleaning protocols.

Ladies and gentlemen!! This is a matter of hygiene and can create diseases.

You have not learned anything from the pandemic!!!

Reflecting pools/fountains poorly maintained.. Really disappointed! We came here (from Los Angles, California) 2 years ago. It was two months prior to the 2024 Olympics. The reflecting pools surrounding the pyramid were under major construction, to fix them up for the Olympic tourists.

Today, May 16, 2026, we are in Paris again and went to see how the revamping of the pools turned out.

They were dry and apparently had not been used in a long time. There are weeds growing in the cracks and the overflow drains that surround each of the pools are full of trash.

I started this review by writing that I am disappointed; no, change that to disgusted!
How RUDE and arrogant are the staff at the very start of the entry process.. Ignorant, arrogant and down right RUDE staff, horrendous !

A festival is less crowded. Tourist trap.

Delayed opening until 11am, it took an hour to get in. When we managed to get in, it was extremely crowded and hot. All restrooms on the exhibition floors were closed so you had to go back to the main entrance. After standing in line for an hour for the toilet, there was a long line to get back to the exhibition floor. At that point, we decided to leave.
Another strike. Another strike prevented people from entering the Louvre. Keeping visitors waiting for hours in the rain for trade union reasons ... France is struck down

TICKETS REFUND. Booked Louvre tickets 3 months in advance for an international trip, but on arrival we were denied entry for our time slot (before 11am). No prior notice.

Had to buy new tickets on the spot at 3x the price. We were told the original tickets would be refunded—9 months later, still nothing. many emails been sent but till NOTHING!

Very disappointing experience for such a world-famous museum. Poor management and no accountability.
Unable to book tickets on the official website. can't go there, reservation required for this beginning of April and unable to book tickets the site beugue... great ....

Would give 0 stars if I could. Prior to our trip I had constant issues attempting to book the tickets - I tried on multiple devices and eventually it worked on a laptop using safari.

However, due to the technical issues the wrong date/time was sent on the tickets which I didn’t notice as strangely it was the same date and year I booked the tickets on.

On arrival at the museum the queues were huge and slow moving. Then when we tried to scan our tickets we were met with unhelpful and almost aggressive behaviour from the staff. We went to the information desk for assistance and tried to explain there’s issues when booking the tickets and the date/time on the tickets- we weren’t even in Paris so we wouldn’t have chosen them! In the end, due t9 lack of help, we had to pay for tickets again as we had wasted time queuing and our children were keen to see the Mona Lisa.

The museum was over crowded, you couldn’t enjoy the art or experience as it was so busy. To see the Mona Lisa was an unpleasant bum-fight. Staff in the rooms of the exhibitions were disinterested and actually rolled their eyes when we asked for assistance.

The layout seems strange- they expect you to walk the length of the floor, but there is no exit/stairwells at the end, so you have to go all the way back on yourself to go to another area of the museums. Lifts are very limited too.

Cafe was ok and reasonable- the only ‘good’ thing about the experience.

Toilet queues were ridiculous- very few facilities for the number of people they let in.

All in all I would not recommend going to this museum whilst in Paris, use your time for something else and spend the money on something enjoyable.

Worst Museum Experience in the World. I have visited almost every major museum in the world and the Louvre provides easily the most unpleasant experience of any museum I have visited.

Let’s start with the cost. €32 for non-EU adults is ridiculous. I live in the UK where all major museums (National Gallery, British Museum, Tate Britain, Tate Modern, Natural History Museum and many more) are completely free, including for all EU visitors. Charging 50% more for non-EU visitors is unjustifiable.

Of course you have to book and pay far in advance to get a time slot to enter the Louvre. So we arrived at our pre-paid time slot, only to wait 1.5 hours to get through the security queue! And even then, you’re not yet in the museum - that takes another 30 minutes.

After two hours, you’re finally inside and the museum is totally packed. Why do they sell so many more tickets than the museum can handle? Could it be greed?

Navigating the museum with its illogical layout and poor signage is very unpleasant. Despite getting directions from three different staff members, we never succeeded in finding the Northern European painting section. We finally gave up and left. It was great to be out of this miserable, overcrowded place.

In total contrast, the Musee d’Orsay was a fantastic experience. They have all the great 19th century art that used to be in the Louvre and the building is very attractive and simple to navigate. My strong advice is to skip the Louvre and enjoy yourself at the Musee d’Orsay just across the river (with no additional, punitive cost for non-EU citizens).

The descendants of the monarchs..... Unfortunately, disorganization is becoming more comfortable.

Knowing the Louvre well having visited it ten times we decided to go exclusively to discover the new gallery of the five continents.

So we pay 44€ without any notice alerting the customer about possible closures that afternoon!!!

I move with difficulty and we take quite a while to reach this place that we are curious to discover.

Weary! We have not finished visiting the first room that a crime scene-like device with a group of employees walking in line like security agents is put in place to turn back visitors. From the snippets of information that are reluctantly given to us like bones to insistent dogs we understand that an important figure (which implies that everyone present is really nothing more than the despicable fretine menu!!!!!!!!!!) decided (at the last minute??????????) to take a short walk in this place (to show that you are up to date with the cultural activity at a Parisian diner or maybe prove your influence and power to a cousin who lives halfway around the world!!!!) and that there is no question that visitors (probably smelling bad!!!!!) can share this place with the OIL. I translate the message to some shocked tourists and try a verbal defense by explaining that I came only for this place and that I found that the republican principle of Equality was flouted in our case since obviously as in the days of La Fontaine "the reason of the strongest was always the best", I vaguely alluded to the fact that nothing announced this hunt for plague victims.

Pain Lost!!!!

As an English friend said to me: "it is that these people know that they are the descendants of the monarchy", everything is said!!!!

Total Ripoff. I had two timed tickets for February 18 and 19, 2026. I got there 15 minutes early. The line was a mile long and it was drizzling. The line didn't move for 45 minutes. I finally got out of line and asked the guard what was going on. She said they were on strike. There was NO NOTICE of any sort. I left.

I came back about 5 and finally got in. NONE of the guards had the same information. What I wanted to see was closed. But, I was told to come back the next day and that they would be opened. NOT TRUE!!

I came back the next day. The EXACT same nonsense. Line at a stand still. The workers were on strike again. The things that I told would be opened were closed.

About the only thing that was opened were the galleries leading to the Mona Lisa.

I live in NYC and we have great art here. There was NO NEED for me to waste my time at the Louvre. I went to other museums and saw wonderful things.

Poor organisation for the numbers of visitors. Timed entry fails because of 90 minutes security bottleneck.. I don't care how famous the paintings are, I would not advise anyone to subject themselves to the seven circles of hell that is The Louvre. Even if you buy a timed entry ticket, be prepared to wait 90 MINUTES to get through security because they only have ONE X-Ray machine. (School parties just get waved on through). When you are inside, the signage is poor, and some of the best paintings are in galleries that are closed. Don't expect to get anywhere near the Mona Lisa - there will be 10 rows of other people blocking your view. The cafés are badly signposted, queues are way too long, and although the food is good (it is everywhere in Paris) expect to pay double what you expected. The toilets are disgusting.

I would not recommend exhausting yourself to visit The Louvre. If you are an art enthusiast you will hate it. If you are a casual tourist you will wonder why you bothered.

Appalling Service, Rude Staff, and Ruined Experience. To the Management of the Louvre Customer Service Team,

I am writing to you with immense frustration and deep disappointment regarding the completely unacceptable treatment my group and I received at the Louvre on March 22nd, around 13:30h. What was supposed to be a memorable cultural experience was entirely ruined by your incompetent systems and shockingly rude staff.

Despite having perfectly valid tickets, we were aggressively denied entry. The reason? All of our tickets were saved on a single mobile phone. Your staff demanded that we distribute the tickets to every single person's device before we could enter.

This absurd demand was made impossible by the fact that mobile data connection in that area is practically nonexistent, and the free Wi-Fi provided by the Louvre is absolutely awful. We were literally trapped—unable to download or send the tickets, and completely unsupported by your team.

Instead of helping us, the staff members were dismissive, uncommunicative, and incredibly disrespectful. They continuously repeated that it was "not their problem" and simply waved us away from the queue as if we were a nuisance. When I explicitly stated that I would be filing a formal complaint and asked the desk attendant for her name, she outright refused to provide it. However, I believe her name was Anne, and I was informed the manager on duty was Valerie Benot. A management team that allows this kind of hostile environment should be ashamed.

To make matters worse, this was not our only issue with your ticketing system. One of our purchases did not even have the tickets attached to the confirmation email. I was forced to wait in a completely separate queue for 30 minutes, practically begging for the tickets I had already paid for.

Time at a museum as vast as the Louvre is incredibly precious, and we had no intention of wasting ours dealing with incompetent managing staff, broken ticketing systems, and outright rudeness.

I demand a thorough explanation as to why this arbitrary and impossible ticket distribution rule was enforced when your own internet infrastructure cannot support it. Furthermore, I expect to hear exactly how you plan to compensate us for the severe distress, wasted time, and the extremely bad experience we were subjected to.

I look forward to your prompt response and a satisfactory resolution to this miserable experience.

Terrible unwelcome. Horrible experience at the Louvre

Porte Sully 13 March 2pm

No explanation on how to receive a group (I am a teacher and I accompany a class of 1st 16/17 years) I am given the ticket and basta

My students go into the museum

I think I’ve lost some and I turn around and go back to the reception.

I'm coming back to come in...

The manager forbids me to enter and asks me to leave my class alone in the museum

We met 2/3 very unpleasant people ...

The museum is beautiful but I would not go back ...

Masterpieces, but a deplorable organization. This review was rejected on Google!

This necessarily questions!

The museum and the collections are unheard of. Of course!

Too bad there's some pretty crippling false notes:

We are asked to be courteous to the staff who are not! It's a big deal. At the distribution of the audio guides, a jaded and very unpleasant young woman who obviously had no desire to be there.

In the toilet: no hooks and a very relative cleanliness.

The hours seem lunar to me: closing at 6pm on a Saturday. For the largest museum in the world it's quite small...

The plan is unclear and the orientation is difficult.

The wait is endless despite the purchase of tickets in advance.

As a result, the organisation and training of staff needs to be reviewed.

Triumphant Wokism at the 5 Continents Gallery. Triumph of wokism in the Galerie des 5 Continents, which Laurence des Cars boasts in the magazine Nouvelle Galerie. So much money to transform a perfect museography into a formless mess: 'dialogues' (word-mantra of the curators today) very unlikely between works having nothing to do with each other, whose only cartels close by are the provenances (as if they were auctioned!), and descriptive cartels 20 m away. This means that we must be extremely motivated to persevere.

Humor to mark the geographies: Palenque ... we tell you that Mexico is between the United States and Latin America. Everything is at this zero level of culture and education ..... at the Louvre!

Instead of educating its audience, the museum puts itself at the level of its most ignorant visitor, and offers him a me-too of Disneyland.

We really will not regret Madame des Cars!!

Bad experience. Despite booking we did 2 hours and a half queue. We had booked Orsay museum at 3.30pm and due to the wait we were left an hour and a half to visit Louvre.

We lost 15 minutes looking for Hammurabi's code, asked 5 security guards and only one knew the room was locked and the exhibit unvisitable

3 employees understood only French, 2 were annoyed because we disturbed them (they were focused on their smartphone)

The Mona Lisa is visible from afar cause crowd. Of course there are remarkable works, too bad we did not have time to see them. Bad organization for an internationally renowned museum: despite thousands of visitors ONLY a metal detector! SHAMEFUL

No communication. Hello,

The Louvre is regularly closed due to a strike by museum employees.

Visitors are in the queue for hours, no info... and no opening of doors.

On Google there is no info and Google reviews are blocked

to prevent communication.

Regards

SHAMEFUL TO SUCK!. SHAMEFUL!!! February 19, tickets bought online, slots at 10:00: we arrive at the Louvre and a tiny sign with reception boys say that there is an employee strike, yet another, and that they do not know if and when the museum will open. "MAYBE YOU'LL STAND IN LINE FOR NOTHING." Thousands of people lined up in the rain, inexplicable and inconclusive lines everywhere, thousands of people lost, we stood in line for about three hours in the rain then, exhausted, we left. Meanwhile, we saw the employees leaving the museum. NONE of them or the executives, who were not there in the rain, warned us or gave notice of a possible opening. SHAME ON YOU, APOLOGIZE AND COMPENSATE FOR TICKETS PAID FOR NOTHING. YOU SUCK!
Booking scam. Online booking for 12:30 on February 18. Arrival at 12:15. The tail of 11h not gone!!! Several hours of waiting

A disgrace, a French scandal!. A scandal! Entrance reserved for 2:30 p.m., entrance to the queue 2 p.m., entrance to the museum... 16:30! Only one security checkpoint mixing individuals and groups (who pass first) and still we were at the Louvre carousel, outside people were waiting in the rain! One of the greatest museums in the world and a lamentable organization! A disgrace to France! PS: And why is the Mona Lisa hanging so low?

Never again. This was the worst part of our Paris trip.

We were made to wait for an hour in the pouring rain, without explanation for the delay (we had a timed entry). Personnel were unhelpful. The lockers didn’t work or were full. The audio guide is not worth the money, there are only a handful of explanations. All in all - never again

Be prepared to suffer. It was the worst thing we did. You have a timed ticket but still need to queue for an hour and a quarter because they've only forked out for one security scanner. We are in our sixties and were stressed and exhausted by the time we got in. The thing we wanted to see, the African, Asian and Oceania art was closed today. So we left after half zn hour most of which we spent sitting down recovering from the queue. I swear, I'm never going to the Louvre again.
Zero expectation. It tells you 45 minutes waiting instead of you arriving at the time of your ticket and in the end you return at 4 pm at 2:30 pm

Horrible!!!!!!. Tour guides are thieves scammers, dirty building, no security and hyper expensive

Go see it on the internet!!!!

It’ll save 3 hours waiting in the rain

Terrible customer service. I hope its nothing like Google, as the museum seems to be able to stop bad reviews, the booking system is horrendous, deleting information starting over again, in error & frustration we booked the wrong day, contacted the museum immediately, they refused to change the tickets or refund, literally ruining our grandsons trip to Paris as we can't afford to pay twice, terrible customer service

Deplorable. Exercising the right to strike is audible. Not informing visitors is unacceptable and a total lack of respect. The museum is marked as open. Poor security guard left to his own devices not able to answer. Deplorable. Monday 26 January 2026

Wild strike at the Louvre😡. Today, January 19, I had a visit booked at 3pm.

At 12 noon an email arrives informing me that the lauvre is closed for strike and that the tickets will be refunded.

Tomorrow the museum is closed for rest and I'm going home on Wednesday.

Great service😬 thank goodness that the Italians according to the French are disorganized, at least in us the strikes are announced with a minimum of notice, out of respect for the user even if appropriately not the manager"

Long live Italy!

Museum on strike: ticket not refunded!. What a disappointment!

On December 14, I book an entry to the exhibition "Jacques-Louis David" on Wednesday, December 17.

That day, I learn that the museum that was closed the day before due to strike is "partially" open. No details are given of this partial opening. I was waiting to be refunded my ticket as it seemed obvious to me that the announcement of a partial opening without specifying could not be opposed to not refund me. Well! It was a mistake on my part because I didn't get a refund.

Another reason for dissatisfaction was that I had booked my ticket and created an account on the ticketlouvre.fr website and received my ticket at the email address used to create my account. When I want to log in to the site again, I receive the message "Unknown or invalid email address. Please check this address or register an account." However, it is the one on which I received my ticket!

Conclusion: I deplore the poor image this museum gives to people who have probably experienced the same disappointment as me (as if its image was not already tarnished enough!)

Rude Employee. I was really disappointed by my visit to the Louvre, specifically because of an interaction I had with one of the information staff. On Nov 13th Thursday morning around 9 a.m., I approached an older gentleman at the information desk with what I thought was a simple question — where to get the audio guide.

Instead of giving me a direct answer, he just pointed toward the ticket booth. Wanting to be sure I understood correctly, I asked for clarification about whether the audio guides were sold there. Rather than confirming it, he responded with, “You are asking the same question twice,” and gave me a frown.

I found the tone and attitude unnecessarily unfriendly, especially for a museum that welcomes so many visitors from around the world. A little courtesy and clear communication would have made a big difference. The museum itself is incredible, but this interaction left a sour note at the start of my visit.

Not everyone is welcomed. Unfortunately, the Louvre is not designed for people with diverse needs. The museum is enormous, extremely crowded, with long queues and a lot of noise. But there is no opportunity to rest in a quiet place where you can have some water and a snack. I have a child with autism.
